questions.stem: A 58-year-old man presents with right knee pain following prolonged yard work. He denies fever, chills, or recent trauma. Examination shows crepitus on passive flexion, bony enlargement of the joint margins, and pain with end-range motion. There is no effusion and no warmth. Which clinical finding most strongly supports osteoarthritis as the primary diagnosis?

options.A: Morning stiffness lasting 2.5 hours
options.B: Bilateral symmetric involvement with metacarpophalangeal joint tenderness
options.C: Crepitus, bony enlargement, and morning stiffness lasting less than 30 minutes
options.D: Extreme tenderness with low-grade fever and joint effusion

questions.rationale: Osteoarthritis is characterized by crepitus, bony enlargement (osteophyte formation), pain with motion, and morning stiffness lasting less than 30 minutes — activity-related stiffness that resolves quickly distinguishes it from inflammatory arthropathies. Option A is incorrect: stiffness of 2.5 hours is typical of rheumatoid arthritis (>60 minutes is the RA threshold) or other inflammatory joint disease. Option B is incorrect: bilateral symmetric MCP involvement is a hallmark of rheumatoid arthritis, not OA. Option D is incorrect: extreme tenderness with fever and effusion raises concern for septic arthritis or acute crystal arthropathy, not OA.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old female soccer player reports anterior knee pain that worsens when walking down stairs and after sitting for 90 minutes in a lecture. She has no history of trauma, swelling, or giving way. Examination reveals no effusion, normal ligament stability, and pain with compression of the patella against the femur. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.A: Anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) tear
options.B: Patellofemoral pain syndrome (PFPS)
options.C: Medial meniscal tear
options.D: Prepatellar bursitis

questions.rationale: Patellofemoral pain syndrome (PFPS) classically presents in young active individuals with anterior knee pain worsened by stair descent, squatting, and prolonged sitting (the 'movie sign'). Pain with patellar compression (Clarke's test) without effusion or instability is characteristic. Option A is incorrect: ACL tears present with acute hemarthrosis, a 'pop' sensation, and positive anterior drawer or Lachman test. Option C is incorrect: meniscal tears cause joint line tenderness, positive McMurray test, and often mechanical symptoms (locking, catching). Option D is incorrect: prepatellar bursitis presents with a discrete fluctuant swelling directly over the patella, not diffuse anterior pain worsened by sitting.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with hypertension, CKD stage 3b (eGFR 38), and bilateral knee OA presents for routine follow-up. He rates his knee pain 6/10 and requests medication for better control. Current medications include amlodipine and lisinopril. He uses ibuprofen 400mg three times daily PRN with moderate relief. Which analgesic adjustment is most appropriate?

options.A: Continue ibuprofen and add omeprazole for GI protection
options.B: Switch ibuprofen to celecoxib 200mg daily
options.C: Switch ibuprofen to topical diclofenac 1% gel applied to both knees TID
options.D: Prescribe tramadol 50mg TID as a safer renal alternative

questions.rationale: Topical diclofenac gel is the preferred analgesic for this patient because it provides effective local analgesia for knee OA with minimal systemic absorption, avoiding the renal, cardiovascular, and GI risks of systemic NSAIDs. With CKD stage 3b and concurrent ACEi (lisinopril), systemic NSAIDs pose a 'triple whammy' risk: reduced GFR, impaired prostaglandin-mediated renal afferent arteriolar tone, and inhibited aldosterone — together causing acute kidney injury. Option A is incorrect: adding a PPI does not address the nephrotoxic and cardiovascular risk of systemic ibuprofen in CKD. Option B is incorrect: celecoxib is still a systemic NSAID and carries the same renal and CV risk profile; it reduces GI risk but not renal or cardiovascular risk. Option D is incorrect: tramadol is a weak opioid and serotonergic agent with its own risks in elderly patients — sedation, fall risk, serotonin syndrome potential — and is not appropriate as first escalation.

questions.stem: A 67-year-old man presents with sudden onset severe left knee pain and swelling after waking from sleep. He has no fever but reports prior similar episodes involving his great toe. Synovial fluid analysis shows positively birefringent rhomboid-shaped crystals. X-ray shows chondrocalcinosis of the medial meniscus. Which medication is most appropriate for acute management?

options.A: Allopurinol 100mg daily started immediately
options.B: Colchicine 0.6mg BID for 3 days
options.C: Doxycycline 100mg BID for 7 days
options.D: Intravenous vancomycin and piperacillin-tazobactam

questions.rationale: This presentation is consistent with pseudogout (CPPD disease): acute crystal arthritis with positively birefringent rhomboid crystals (distinguishing from gout's negatively birefringent needle-shaped crystals), chondrocalcinosis on imaging, and acute onset in an older adult. Management of acute CPPD mirrors gout: colchicine, NSAIDs, or corticosteroids. Option A is incorrect: allopurinol targets uric acid reduction for gout prevention — it has no role in CPPD; furthermore, urate-lowering therapy should NEVER be initiated during an acute gout or pseudogout flare (it can prolong or trigger a flare). Option C is incorrect: doxycycline is an antibiotic with no role in crystal arthritis. Option D is incorrect: although IV antibiotics would be appropriate for septic arthritis, the crystal identification on aspirate makes septic arthritis effectively excluded — treating empirically for infection after crystal diagnosis is identified is inappropriate.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with bilateral knee OA has been receiving physical therapy, has reduced her weight by 12 pounds, and uses topical diclofenac with partial relief. She now reports significant functional limitation — unable to walk more than one block without pain. She asks if there is any other option before surgery. On examination, there is a moderate effusion in the right knee and crepitus throughout range of motion. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Refer to orthopedic surgery for bilateral total knee replacement
options.B: Start oral celecoxib 200mg daily and schedule MRI
options.C: Perform intra-articular corticosteroid injection to the right knee and reassess
options.D: Order bone scan to evaluate for avascular necrosis

questions.rationale: This patient has optimized conservative therapy (PT, weight loss, topical NSAIDs) but has ongoing functional limitation and an active effusion — the next appropriate step in the OA stepwise ladder is intra-articular corticosteroid injection. Corticosteroid injections are effective for moderate-to-severe OA with effusion and can provide 4-12 weeks of pain relief, allowing continued function. Option A is incorrect: surgical referral is appropriate when conservative therapy, including injections, has failed — it is premature at this stage; bilateral surgery is particularly high-risk and is not the immediate next step. Option B is incorrect: adding oral celecoxib may help but does not address the active effusion; MRI is not indicated in established OA management — it is for surgical planning or when diagnosis is uncertain. Option D is incorrect: bone scan is not indicated; AVN would present with acute severe pain and risk factors (corticosteroid use, alcohol, sickle cell), not bilateral chronic OA pattern.

questions.stem: A 61-year-old woman presents with right knee pain for 6 months. She describes diffuse pain around the knee without localization. Knee examination is unremarkable: no effusion, no joint line tenderness, full range of motion, normal ligament stability. However, she also mentions right groin pain and difficulty putting on her socks. Hip examination reveals restricted internal rotation and reproduced groin and right thigh discomfort. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Order MRI of the right knee
options.B: Refer to orthopedics for knee arthroscopy evaluation
options.C: Order AP pelvis and frog-leg lateral X-ray to evaluate the right hip
options.D: Prescribe topical NSAIDs for presumed early knee OA

questions.rationale: This is a classic presentation of referred knee pain from hip OA. The key discriminating finding is a completely normal knee examination with restricted hip internal rotation and reproduced groin/thigh pain on hip provocation. Hip OA commonly refers pain to the anterior knee and medial thigh. The next step is imaging the hip, not the knee. Option A is incorrect: MRI of the knee is not indicated when the knee examination is entirely normal; it would likely be negative and delay the correct diagnosis. Option B is incorrect: arthroscopy without adequate imaging or a positive knee examination would be premature and potentially harmful. Option D is incorrect: treating the knee with topical NSAIDs without identifying the source would fail — the pain is hip-generated and requires hip-directed management.

questions.stem: Which of the following patients meets criteria for uncomplicated UTI and can be treated without urine culture?

options.A: A 28-year-old non-pregnant woman with 2-day history of dysuria and frequency, no fever, no prior UTI in 3 months
options.B: A 55-year-old man with new-onset dysuria and urinary frequency
options.C: A 32-year-old pregnant woman with urinary frequency and positive urine dipstick
options.D: A 68-year-old woman with T2DM and dysuria, two UTIs in the last 6 months

questions.rationale: Uncomplicated UTI is defined as acute lower urinary tract infection in a non-pregnant, structurally normal, immunocompetent woman. The 28-year-old meets all criteria — healthy female, non-pregnant, no structural abnormalities, and short symptom duration — and can be treated empirically without culture. Option B is incorrect: UTI in a male is by definition complicated (anatomical factors, prostatitis risk) and requires urine culture. Option C is incorrect: pregnant patients always require culture-guided treatment for UTI or ASB regardless of complication status. Option D is incorrect: diabetes, female with recurrent UTI (2 episodes in 6 months), and older age classify this as complicated — culture is mandatory.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old non-pregnant woman presents with dysuria and frequency for 2 days. She has no drug allergies. Local resistance data shows E. coli TMP-SMX resistance at 28%. She has normal renal function. What is the most appropriate first-line antibiotic for uncomplicated cystitis?

options.A: TMP-SMX DS BID for 3 days
options.B: Nitrofurantoin monohydrate/macrocrystals 100mg BID for 5 days
options.C: Ciprofloxacin 250mg BID for 3 days
options.D: Cephalexin 500mg QID for 7 days

questions.rationale: Nitrofurantoin monohydrate/macrocrystals (Macrobid) is the preferred first-line agent for uncomplicated cystitis: excellent E. coli coverage, minimal collateral damage to gut flora, and low resistance rates. It is the correct choice when local resistance data precludes TMP-SMX. Option A is incorrect: TMP-SMX resistance at 28% exceeds the 20% threshold above which empirical use is not recommended without culture guidance — using TMP-SMX here risks treatment failure. Option C is incorrect: ciprofloxacin (fluoroquinolone) is specifically contraindicated as first-line for uncomplicated cystitis due to high collateral damage (microbiome disruption, Clostridioides difficile risk, tendinopathy) and preservation of this class for complicated infections. Option D is incorrect: cephalexin has variable activity against common uropathogens and is not recommended as first-line; it lacks the evidence base of nitrofurantoin or TMP-SMX.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old woman at 14 weeks gestation presents for her first prenatal visit. Urinalysis dipstick shows nitrite positive and leukocyte esterase 2+. She has no urinary symptoms. Urine culture is sent and returns positive for >100,000 CFU/mL of E. coli. She has no drug allergies. Which is the most appropriate management?

options.A: Observe without treatment; recheck culture in 4 weeks
options.B: Treat with nitrofurantoin 100mg BID for 5 days and reschedule follow-up in 1 month
options.C: Treat with TMP-SMX DS BID for 3 days as the safest option in pregnancy
options.D: Treat with ciprofloxacin 500mg BID for 3 days for the most reliable coverage

questions.rationale: Asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnancy must always be treated — it significantly increases the risk of pyelonephritis (25-fold), preterm labor, and low birth weight. At 14 weeks (second trimester), nitrofurantoin is safe and appropriate. Option A is incorrect: observation without treatment is acceptable only in non-pregnant adults; in pregnancy, ASB treatment is mandatory regardless of symptoms. Option C is incorrect: TMP-SMX is contraindicated in the first trimester (folate antagonism, neural tube defect risk) and is not recommended near term (potential kernicterus risk) — at 14 weeks, it is generally avoided in preference for safer alternatives. Option D is incorrect: fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin) are contraindicated in pregnancy due to theoretical risk of fetal cartilage damage.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old woman with mild dementia is brought to clinic by her daughter, who reports that the patient has been confused for 2 days. Vital signs: T 37.1°C, BP 128/74, HR 82. Urinalysis: cloudy urine, nitrites positive, WBC 12/hpf, bacteria moderate. Urine culture is pending. She is otherwise at her functional baseline except for the cognitive changes. Which is the best next step?

options.A: Start nitrofurantoin 100mg BID for 5 days empirically for UTI-associated delirium
options.B: Hold antibiotics, perform a systematic delirium workup, and review medications for culprits
options.C: Start ciprofloxacin 500mg BID for 7 days for complicated UTI with altered mental status
options.D: Send patient to the emergency department for presumed urosepsis

questions.rationale: This patient has bacteriuria on urinalysis but no systemic features of UTI (no fever, no UTI-localized symptoms in a patient unable to self-report). Bacteriuria is ubiquitous in older women — over 50% of institutionalized women have positive urine cultures without UTI. Confusion in an elderly patient with positive UA is frequently attributed to UTI without adequate evaluation, which leads to antibiotic overuse and misses the true cause (medication side effect, dehydration, early pneumonia, acute metabolic change). A systematic delirium evaluation should precede treatment. Option A is incorrect: empirically treating without confirming UTI as the cause perpetuates the inappropriate UTI-delirium conflation. Option C is incorrect: ciprofloxacin is not first-line and there is insufficient evidence this represents UTI-related AMS without localizing signs. Option D is incorrect: she is hemodynamically stable without fever or tachycardia — there is no indication for emergency referral at this time.

questions.stem: A 31-year-old non-pregnant woman presents with 3 days of dysuria, left-sided flank pain, fever (38.8°C), and vomiting. She is unable to keep fluids down. She has no drug allergies and her creatinine is 0.9 mg/dL. Blood pressure is 102/68, heart rate 104. Which management plan is most appropriate?

options.A: Prescribe oral ciprofloxacin 500mg BID for 7 days and follow up in 48-72 hours
options.B: Prescribe nitrofurantoin 100mg BID for 5 days and follow up in 48-72 hours
options.C: Send a urine culture, prescribe antiemetics, and follow up in 24 hours
options.D: Transfer to emergency department for IV antibiotics and hydration

questions.rationale: This patient has pyelonephritis with systemic illness (fever, tachycardia, hypotension, and vomiting preventing oral intake). She cannot safely be managed with oral antibiotics because she cannot absorb them. Patients with pyelonephritis who cannot tolerate oral medications, have hemodynamic instability (borderline hypotension, tachycardia), or who appear systemically ill require hospital-level care with IV antibiotics and hydration. Option A is incorrect: oral ciprofloxacin is appropriate for outpatient pyelonephritis in a stable patient who can tolerate oral intake — this patient cannot. Option B is incorrect: nitrofurantoin has poor tissue penetration and is specifically contraindicated for pyelonephritis regardless of renal function; it is only appropriate for lower UTI. Option C is incorrect: sending cultures without beginning treatment and observing without antipyretics or antibiotics is unsafe in a febrile, hemodynamically borderline patient.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old postmenopausal woman reports her fourth uncomplicated UTI in the past 10 months. Cultures confirm E. coli sensitive to nitrofurantoin each time. She denies incontinence, structural symptoms, or incomplete bladder emptying. She drinks water regularly and voids after intercourse. Which is the most evidence-based prevention strategy for this patient?

options.A: Recommend daily cranberry supplements 500mg
options.B: Prescribe trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole DS daily continuously for 12 months
options.C: Prescribe topical vaginal estrogen cream
options.D: Refer to urology for cystoscopy to evaluate for structural abnormality

questions.rationale: In postmenopausal women, vaginal atrophy from estrogen deficiency disrupts normal vaginal flora and creates a favorable environment for uropathogens. Topical vaginal estrogen is the most evidence-based non-antibiotic strategy for recurrent UTI prevention in this population — it restores Lactobacillus colonization and significantly reduces UTI recurrence. It has minimal systemic absorption and is safe in patients who cannot use systemic hormone therapy. Option A is incorrect: cranberry supplements have consistently failed to show benefit in high-quality randomized trials for UTI prevention. Option B is incorrect: continuous antibiotic prophylaxis (TMP-SMX) is a valid second-line option for recurrent UTI but should follow a trial of non-antibiotic prevention in a patient who has not yet tried vaginal estrogen; it also carries resistance risk. Option D is incorrect: structural evaluation is appropriate after recurrent complicated UTI or failure of all prevention strategies, not as first-line management for straightforward recurrent uncomplicated UTI.

questions.stem: A patient with T2DM on maximally tolerated metformin has a current A1c of 7.8%. His individualized A1c target is 7.0%. He has no ASCVD, HF, or CKD. He is 54 years old with BMI 33 and wants to avoid hypoglycemia. Which agent class is most appropriate to add?

options.A: Insulin glargine for most reliable A1c reduction
options.B: Sulfonylurea (glipizide) for high glycemic potency and low cost
options.C: DPP-4 inhibitor (sitagliptin) for weight neutrality and no hypoglycemia risk
options.D: GLP-1 receptor agonist (semaglutide) for A1c reduction combined with weight loss benefit

questions.rationale: In a patient without specific cardiovascular or renal indication but with obesity (BMI 33) and preference to avoid hypoglycemia, a GLP-1 receptor agonist provides the best combination of A1c reduction (1.5-2.0%), significant weight loss benefit (3-6kg), and no intrinsic hypoglycemia risk. Option A is incorrect: insulin provides reliable glycemic control but carries significant hypoglycemia risk, requires injection and monitoring training, and causes weight gain — inappropriate as first intensification step in this profile. Option B is incorrect: sulfonylureas carry hypoglycemia risk (especially with missed meals), promote weight gain, and are associated with cardiovascular risk signals — not aligned with this patient's stated preference. Option C is incorrect: DPP-4 inhibitors are weight neutral and hypoglycemia-free but provide only 0.6-0.8% A1c reduction — insufficient for a 0.8% gap; GLP-1 RA is superior in potency and provides additional weight benefit.

questions.stem: A 61-year-old woman with T2DM, A1c 8.2% on metformin, is found to have new-onset HFrEF (EF 35%) on echocardiogram. She has no history of recurrent genitourinary infections. eGFR is 68. Which second agent is most appropriate to add?

options.A: Saxagliptin 5mg daily for low hypoglycemia risk
options.B: Pioglitazone 30mg daily for insulin sensitization
options.C: Dapagliflozin 10mg daily for HF benefit and glycemic control
options.D: Semaglutide 0.5mg weekly for ASCVD and weight benefit

questions.rationale: SGLT-2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin) are the preferred second agent when T2DM coexists with HFrEF — they reduce hospitalizations for heart failure and cardiovascular death, and this benefit is independent of glycemic status. Dapagliflozin has specific FDA approval for HFrEF regardless of diabetes status. eGFR 68 is well above the threshold for initiation. Option A is incorrect: saxagliptin is specifically contraindicated (or strongly discouraged) in HFrEF — the SAVOR-TIMI trial showed a 27% increase in hospitalization for heart failure with saxagliptin; it must be avoided. Option B is incorrect: pioglitazone causes sodium and water retention, exacerbating heart failure — it is contraindicated in HF (NYHA Class 3-4) and should be avoided across all HF stages. Option D is incorrect: semaglutide (GLP-1 RA) has demonstrated MACE reduction in ASCVD but does NOT reduce HHF — for a patient whose primary complication is HFrEF, SGLT-2i is the evidence-based first choice.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with T2DM, CKD stage 3b (eGFR 38), and a recent MI has an A1c of 8.5%. He is currently on metformin (held pending creatinine review), aspirin, atorvastatin, and carvedilol. He has no history of HF. Which agent combination is most appropriate for intensification?

options.A: Glipizide IR plus linagliptin
options.B: Empagliflozin 10mg plus semaglutide SC weekly
options.C: Glyburide plus sitagliptin (adjusted for CKD)
options.D: Pioglitazone plus GLP-1 receptor agonist

questions.rationale: With established ASCVD (post-MI) and CKD stage 3b, empagliflozin provides both cardiovascular and renal protection — it reduces MACE, HHF, and slows CKD progression. It can be initiated down to eGFR ≥20 for its cardiovascular and renal indications (glycemic benefit diminishes <45). Adding semaglutide addresses ASCVD-related MACE reduction further and provides additional A1c lowering. Option A is incorrect: while linagliptin (no renal adjustment) is appropriate in CKD, glipizide carries hypoglycemia risk and offers no cardiovascular protection — this combination does not address the ASCVD or renal priorities. Option C is incorrect: glyburide is a long-acting sulfonylurea explicitly listed in the Beers Criteria and specifically contraindicated in CKD due to active metabolite accumulation causing prolonged hypoglycemia. Sitagliptin requires dose reduction at CrCl <50, not full dose. Option D is incorrect: pioglitazone causes fluid retention and may worsen cardiac status in post-MI patients; it is generally avoided in significant cardiovascular disease.

questions.stem: A 47-year-old woman with T2DM has been on empagliflozin 10mg and metformin for 9 months. She presents for a pre-surgical evaluation for elective knee replacement scheduled in 5 days. Her current A1c is 6.9%, fasting glucose 118 mg/dL. Which pre-operative adjustment is most appropriate?

options.A: No adjustment needed; continue empagliflozin through the surgical date
options.B: Stop empagliflozin 3 days before surgery and restart after she is eating
options.C: Switch from empagliflozin to glipizide IR for the peri-operative period
options.D: Hospitalize 2 days before surgery for IV insulin bridging

questions.rationale: SGLT-2 inhibitors must be held before major surgery — typically 3 days before for elective procedures — due to the risk of euglycemic diabetic ketoacidosis (eudka), which can occur with surgical stress, fasting, or reduced carbohydrate intake. Euglycemic DKA is characterized by ketonemia and metabolic acidosis with a normal or minimally elevated blood glucose, making it easily missed if not anticipated. SGLT-2 inhibitors should be restarted only when the patient is eating normally. Option A is incorrect: continuing SGLT-2i through surgery is a patient safety error — peri-operative fasting and surgical stress significantly increase eudka risk. Option C is incorrect: switching to glipizide introduces hypoglycemia risk in a peri-operative NPO patient; this is not appropriate bridging. Option D is incorrect: IV insulin bridging is reserved for poorly controlled diabetes (A1c >9%) or insulin-dependent patients — this patient's diabetes is well-controlled and does not require hospitalization.

questions.stem: A 79-year-old woman with T2DM, moderate dementia, and a recent hip fracture (now residing in a care facility) has an A1c of 8.9%. Her care facility nurse notes she frequently misses meals. Her current medications include glipizide 10mg BID. She has had two hypoglycemic episodes in the last month. What is the most appropriate A1c target and medication change for this patient?

options.A: Target A1c <7.0%; add basal insulin glargine at bedtime
options.B: Target A1c <8.5%; discontinue glipizide; start linagliptin 5mg daily
options.C: Target A1c <8.0%; switch glipizide to glimepiride for longer action
options.D: Target A1c <7.5%; continue glipizide and add weekly liraglutide injection

questions.rationale: In a frail elderly patient with cognitive impairment, missed meals, and documented hypoglycemia, the primary harm prevention goal shifts from preventing complications (long-term benefit) to preventing acute hypoglycemic events (immediate safety). ADA guidelines recommend an A1c target of up to 8.5% for frail patients with limited life expectancy and hypoglycemia risk. Glipizide must be discontinued — it is causing the hypoglycemia. Linagliptin is the appropriate replacement: it requires no renal dose adjustment, has no intrinsic hypoglycemia risk, and is available once daily. Option A is incorrect: targeting <7.0% with basal insulin adds further hypoglycemia risk in a patient who misses meals — this would be unsafe and would worsen the existing problem. Option C is incorrect: switching to glimepiride (another long-acting sulfonylurea) addresses the specific drug but not the class problem — all sulfonylureas cause hypoglycemia and are Beers Criteria in elderly patients. Option D is incorrect: weekly GLP-1 RA injection adds complexity (injection training in dementia setting) and GI side effects that worsen nutritional intake; the target <7.5% is still too tight for this patient's risk profile.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with newly diagnosed T2DM presents with A1c 11.2%, polyuria, polydipsia, and a 14-pound weight loss over 3 months. Random glucose is 387 mg/dL. He has no ketonuria. Which initial management strategy is most appropriate?

options.A: Metformin 500mg BID titrated up over 4 weeks, with recheck A1c in 3 months
options.B: Start metformin plus a GLP-1 receptor agonist immediately, target A1c in 6 months
options.C: Initiate basal insulin (glargine) immediately; add metformin once glucose is controlled
options.D: Hospitalize for IV insulin infusion protocol

questions.rationale: A newly diagnosed T2DM patient with A1c >10% and symptomatic hyperglycemia (weight loss, polyuria, polydipsia) requires rapid glucose reduction. Basal insulin is indicated at diagnosis when A1c is significantly above goal with symptomatic hyperglycemia — it allows immediate effective glycemic reduction while metformin is gradually introduced. After stabilization, de-intensification is often possible as beta-cell function recovers (glucose toxicity reverses). Option A is incorrect: starting metformin alone in a patient with A1c 11.2% would take months to show effect — symptomatic hyperglycemia requires faster intervention. Option B is incorrect: while combination therapy is guideline-supported for high A1c, GLP-1 RA onset is gradual (weeks to months titration) and does not address the urgency of the current presentation. Option D is incorrect: IV insulin is reserved for DKA/HHS or inpatient acute hyperglycemic crisis; this patient has no ketonuria, is ambulatory and hemodynamically stable — outpatient basal insulin initiation is appropriate.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old woman on empagliflozin 10mg, metformin, and linagliptin presents to urgent care with nausea, vomiting, and feeling 'off' for 2 days. Vital signs: T 37.2°C, BP 118/72, HR 92. Blood glucose is 168 mg/dL. Labs: serum bicarbonate 14 mEq/L, anion gap 22. Urine ketones large. What is the most appropriate action?

options.A: Reassure the patient that glucose is not critically elevated; recheck glucose in 2 hours
options.B: Diagnose euglycemic DKA, stop empagliflozin, and transfer to emergency department
options.C: Diagnose a gastrointestinal illness and prescribe ondansetron with follow-up in 24 hours
options.D: Increase insulin dosing if patient is on basal insulin, and hydrate orally

questions.rationale: This is euglycemic DKA — a well-recognized but underdiagnosed adverse effect of SGLT-2 inhibitors. The hallmarks are: anion gap metabolic acidosis (anion gap 22, bicarb 14), large ketonuria, and glucose in the normal-to-mildly-elevated range (not the markedly elevated glucose of classic DKA). The absence of markedly elevated glucose can cause dismissal of this diagnosis. The SGLT-2 inhibitor must be stopped immediately and the patient transferred for IV fluids, insulin infusion, and electrolyte management. Option A is incorrect: attributing the presentation to a non-critical glucose ignores the anion gap acidosis and large ketonuria, which together define DKA regardless of glucose level. Option C is incorrect: attributing this to a simple GI illness misses a potentially fatal metabolic derangement — the lab pattern (high anion gap + ketonuria) demands DKA evaluation regardless of the glucose. Option D is incorrect: this patient is not on insulin; the patient does not have insulin-deficient DKA in the classical sense — the mechanism is SGLT-2-mediated ketogenesis; empirically adding insulin without DKA protocol management is inappropriate outpatient management.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old man with a 40 pack-year smoking history presents with progressive dyspnea and morning cough productive of white sputum for 2 years. Spirometry shows post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC of 0.62 and FEV1 72% predicted. His CAT score is 7 and he has had no exacerbations in the past year. How is this patient classified using GOLD 2024?

options.A: GOLD 2, Group A
options.B: GOLD 2, Group B
options.C: GOLD 1, Group E
options.D: GOLD 2, Group E

questions.rationale: GOLD 2024 classification uses two independent axes: spirometric grade (GOLD 1-4 based on FEV1) and ABE group (symptom burden + exacerbation history). FEV1 72% predicted = GOLD Grade 2 (50-79%). CAT score 7 = fewer symptoms (threshold is CAT ≥10 for 'more symptoms'). Zero exacerbations in the past year = Group A. Therefore: GOLD 2, Group A. Option B is incorrect: Group B requires CAT ≥10 or mMRC ≥2 — this patient's CAT is 7, below the threshold. Option C is incorrect: GOLD 1 requires FEV1 ≥80%; this patient's FEV1 is 72%. Option D is incorrect: Group E requires ≥2 moderate exacerbations or ≥1 hospitalization — he had none.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with COPD is classified as GOLD 3, Group B. Her CAT score is 14. She is currently on SABA PRN only. She denies exacerbations in the past year. Blood eosinophil count is 180 cells/µL. Which inhaler regimen is most appropriate?

options.A: Add inhaled corticosteroid (fluticasone) monotherapy
options.B: Add LAMA (tiotropium) monotherapy
options.C: Add LABA (salmeterol) plus ICS (fluticasone) — LABA/ICS combination
options.D: Add LAMA plus LABA combination (dual bronchodilator) as initial maintenance

questions.rationale: GOLD 2024 Group B patients with high symptom burden (CAT 14) should be initiated on LAMA+LABA dual bronchodilator as their initial maintenance regimen. The combination provides superior bronchodilation compared to either agent alone. Option A is incorrect: ICS monotherapy is not recommended in COPD — it provides minimal bronchodilation and increases pneumonia risk; ICS must always be paired with a long-acting bronchodilator. Option B is incorrect: LAMA monotherapy alone would be appropriate for mildly symptomatic Group A or B patients — with a CAT of 14 (significantly symptomatic), dual bronchodilation is warranted from the start. Option C is incorrect: LABA/ICS combination is not the preferred initial step for Group B — it adds ICS without a clear eosinophilic indication (eos 180, below the 300 threshold); LAMA+LABA is preferred as it avoids unnecessary ICS exposure.

questions.stem: A 67-year-old man with COPD on LAMA+LABA continues to have 3 exacerbations per year despite optimal inhaler technique. Blood eosinophil count is 380 cells/µL. He currently smokes. Which modification to his regimen is most appropriate?

options.A: Add montelukast for eosinophilic inflammation
options.B: Switch from LAMA+LABA to LABA+ICS combination
options.C: Add ICS to the existing LAMA+LABA to achieve triple therapy
options.D: Add roflumilast only, without ICS, due to current smoking

questions.rationale: This patient meets both criteria for ICS addition on top of dual bronchodilator: eosinophils ≥300 cells/µL AND frequent exacerbator (Group E on LAMA+LABA). Triple therapy (LAMA+LABA+ICS) is supported by the IMPACT trial, which showed mortality benefit and exacerbation reduction in this phenotype. Option A is incorrect: montelukast has no evidence base in COPD management; it is used for asthma and allergic rhinitis. Option B is incorrect: switching from LAMA+LABA to LABA+ICS would remove the LAMA, which provides important bronchodilation, and is a downgrade of bronchodilator therapy; the correct approach is to add ICS, not replace. Option D is incorrect: roflumilast is a PDE-4 inhibitor indicated for severe COPD with chronic bronchitis and frequent exacerbations, not as the next step before ICS addition; furthermore, smoking reduces ICS efficacy but does not contraindicate its use in eosinophilic frequent exacerbators.

questions.stem: A 63-year-old woman with known COPD presents with increased dyspnea, increased sputum production, and change in sputum color from white to yellow-green over 3 days. Vital signs are stable, SpO2 94% on room air. She meets all three Anthonisen criteria. Which antibiotic is the most appropriate first-line choice?

options.A: Ciprofloxacin 500mg BID for 7 days
options.B: Azithromycin 500mg day 1 then 250mg for 4 days
options.C: Cefdinir 300mg BID for 7 days
options.D: Moxifloxacin 400mg daily for 5 days

questions.rationale: When antibiotics are indicated for COPD exacerbation (Anthonisen criteria met), first-line options are azithromycin, doxycycline, or amoxicillin-clavulanate. Azithromycin covers H. influenzae and S. pneumoniae (dominant COPD exacerbation pathogens) and has anti-inflammatory properties. Option A is incorrect: ciprofloxacin is a respiratory fluoroquinolone with poor streptococcal coverage and is not recommended for community-acquired COPD exacerbations unless Pseudomonas is suspected (typically in structural lung disease, recent hospitalization). Option C is incorrect: while cephalosporins have some coverage, cefdinir is not a guideline-recommended first-line option for COPD exacerbation. Option D is incorrect: moxifloxacin is a broad-spectrum respiratory fluoroquinolone reserved for patients who have failed first-line therapy or have high-risk features — it is not appropriate as initial empiric therapy for a stable outpatient exacerbation.

questions.stem: A 71-year-old man with known severe COPD presents to urgent care with worsening dyspnea. He is placed on supplemental oxygen. His SpO2 is 85% on room air. Within 20 minutes of starting O2 at 4L/min via nasal cannula, SpO2 rises to 97% and he feels better, but he becomes increasingly lethargic. ABG at this point shows pH 7.28, pCO2 68 mmHg. What was the most likely error in management?

options.A: Oxygen delivery was inadequate; should have used 100% non-rebreather mask
options.B: Oral prednisone should have been given before oxygen
options.C: Oxygen was titrated above the target range for known COPD CO2 retainers, suppressing hypoxic drive
options.D: IV magnesium should have been given for bronchospasm relief

questions.rationale: In known or suspected CO2-retaining COPD patients, oxygen therapy must be titrated to a target SpO2 of 88-92%. Achieving SpO2 97% in this population is over-oxygenation: it suppresses the hypoxic respiratory drive that these patients rely on more than healthy lungs, and the resulting hypoventilation leads to worsening CO2 retention and respiratory acidosis. The pH 7.28 and pCO2 68 confirm CO2 narcosis from over-oxygenation. Option A is incorrect: increasing oxygen concentration further would worsen CO2 retention — the error was too much oxygen, not too little. Option B is incorrect: the order of interventions does not explain the CO2 rise — this is a physiologic response to hyperoxia-induced hypoventilation. Option D is incorrect: IV magnesium is used for refractory asthma, not COPD exacerbation — and does not address the physiological cause of the deterioration.

questions.stem: An 82-year-old woman with COPD and mild cognitive impairment has inadequate inhaler technique with her tiotropium DPI device during clinic assessment. Spirometry shows FEV1 38% predicted. Which change is most appropriate to optimize medication delivery?

options.A: Switch to nebulized ipratropium BID as the most reliable delivery method
options.B: Switch from DPI tiotropium to tiotropium soft-mist inhaler (Respimat) and provide technique training
options.C: Add an MDI salbutamol with spacer and discontinue the LAMA
options.D: Prescribe oral theophylline as systemic bronchodilator when inhalers are not feasible

questions.rationale: Soft-mist inhalers (Respimat) produce a slow-moving mist that does not require the high peak inspiratory flow that dry powder inhalers (DPIs) depend on. In elderly patients with severe COPD and poor inspiratory flow, switching from DPI to SMI maintains the same molecule (tiotropium) while dramatically improving pulmonary drug deposition. Option A is incorrect: while nebulized ipratropium is an option, it requires equipment and setup at home; switching to tiotropium SMI maintains the once-daily convenience with improved delivery. Option C is incorrect: removing the LAMA and replacing with PRN SABA would represent a downgrade in maintenance therapy — SABA does not replace long-acting maintenance bronchodilation. Option D is incorrect: theophylline has a narrow therapeutic index with significant toxicity risk, requires blood level monitoring, and has significant drug interactions — it is rarely used in modern COPD management and should not be chosen before optimizing inhaler device selection.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with COPD (GOLD 3, Group E) presents with a severe exacerbation treated with oral prednisone 40mg and azithromycin for 72 hours at home. He calls to report he is not improving — his dyspnea is 8/10, he cannot complete sentences, and his SpO2 via home oximeter reads 86%. His wife is with him. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Add ipratropium MDI to his current regimen and recheck in 12 hours
options.B: Increase prednisone dose to 60mg and extend antibiotic course by 3 days
options.C: Direct transfer to the emergency department for potential hospitalization
options.D: Schedule urgent pulmonology appointment for the following morning

questions.rationale: This patient has failed outpatient exacerbation therapy at 72 hours with worsening severity: SpO2 86% (below safe outpatient threshold), inability to speak in full sentences, and clinical distress. GOLD criteria for hospital admission include failure of outpatient therapy, SpO2 <88-90% (or worsening), inability to tolerate oral medications (already taking antibiotics and steroids without response), and significant dyspnea at rest. He requires emergency evaluation for non-invasive ventilation, IV therapy, and close monitoring. Option A is incorrect: adding ipratropium MDI as a next outpatient step is dangerously inadequate for a patient with SpO2 86% and severe dyspnea at home. Option B is incorrect: escalating prednisone to 60mg prolongs treatment without benefit (REDUCE trial showed no benefit from longer courses) and does not address the severity of the current deterioration. Option D is incorrect: a 'next morning' appointment is unsafe — this patient's clinical status requires emergency evaluation now, not in 12-16 hours.

questions.stem: A 44-year-old man presents with what he describes as 'the worst headache of his life,' which began suddenly while lifting weights and reached maximum intensity within 45 seconds. He is alert and oriented but uncomfortable. Vital signs are within normal limits. He has never had a headache like this before. What is the highest-priority diagnosis to exclude?

options.A: Exercise-induced migraine
options.B: Subarachnoid hemorrhage
options.C: Tension-type headache triggered by Valsalva
options.D: Cluster headache first presentation

questions.rationale: A 'thunderclap' headache — reaching maximal intensity within seconds — is subarachnoid hemorrhage until proven otherwise. This is a neurological emergency. The classic sentinel headache from SAH is frequently misdiagnosed as migraine or tension-type on first presentation, leading to catastrophic delayed diagnosis. Option A is incorrect: exercise-induced migraine is a diagnosis of exclusion — it cannot be assigned before SAH has been excluded, especially with first-ever maximal-severity headache. Option C is incorrect: Valsalva-related tension headache is benign but a diagnosis of exclusion — the thunderclap onset pattern mandates emergent evaluation regardless of any proposed benign explanation. Option D is incorrect: cluster headache produces severe unilateral periorbital pain but not thunderclap onset within seconds; it also features autonomic symptoms (lacrimation, ptosis) that are absent here.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman presents with a new temporal headache, fatigue, and jaw pain when eating for 3 weeks. She has no fever or visual symptoms today but reports one episode of 'blurring' in her right eye 5 days ago that resolved after 30 minutes. ESR is 84 mm/hr, CRP is 4.2 mg/dL. Which action is most critical to initiate today in the primary care or urgent care setting?

options.A: Order temporal artery duplex ultrasound and schedule biopsy within 2 weeks
options.B: Start prednisone 40-60mg daily immediately, then arrange temporal artery biopsy
options.C: Refer to ophthalmology for same-day exam before starting any treatment
options.D: Start ibuprofen 600mg TID while awaiting rheumatology referral

questions.rationale: This presentation is classic giant cell arteritis (GCA): age >50, new temporal headache, jaw claudication, elevated ESR and CRP, and a transient visual episode consistent with amaurosis fugax — a warning sign before permanent ischemic optic neuropathy. High-dose corticosteroids must be started IMMEDIATELY when GCA is clinically suspected. Every hour of delay increases the risk of permanent blindness. Temporal artery biopsy can be performed within 2 weeks of starting steroids without significantly reducing diagnostic yield. Option A is incorrect: waiting for biopsy before treatment is dangerous — if GCA causes optic artery occlusion during the wait, vision loss is permanent and irreversible. Option C is incorrect: same-day ophthalmology is appropriate and should be arranged, but treatment must not be withheld awaiting the exam — steroids come first. Option D is incorrect: ibuprofen has no effect on vasculitis; treating GCA with NSAIDs without steroids allows the disease to progress to visual loss.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman presents to urgent care with severe right-sided headache and right eye pain. On examination, you note right ptosis, right eye deviated down and out, and the right pupil is dilated and non-reactive to light. She reports this came on suddenly 2 hours ago. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Prescribe sumatriptan 6mg SC for presumed ocular migraine with third-nerve aura
options.B: Refer urgently to ophthalmology for management of right cranial nerve III palsy
options.C: Call 911 for immediate transfer; this presentation is consistent with a posterior communicating artery aneurysm compressing CN III
options.D: Obtain urgent CT head to evaluate for ischemic stroke involving the brainstem

questions.rationale: A painful third cranial nerve palsy with pupil involvement (dilated, non-reactive) is a posterior communicating artery (PComm) aneurysm emergency until proven otherwise. The pupillomotor fibers of CN III run on the outside of the nerve and are compressed first by an expanding aneurysm. This is a pre-rupture sentinel event — if the aneurysm ruptures, the patient will have a SAH. This is a neurosurgical emergency requiring immediate transfer. Option A is incorrect: treating as migraine is potentially fatal — a painful third nerve palsy with mydriasis is NEVER a migraine variant until PComm aneurysm has been excluded by CTA or MRA; sumatriptan is contraindicated and irrelevant here. Option B is incorrect: routine ophthalmology referral is inadequate for a suspected ruptured or expanding intracranial aneurysm — neurosurgical evaluation is needed immediately. Option D is incorrect: while CT head is the first step in the emergency department workup, ischemic brainstem stroke does not cause a painful mydriatic CN III palsy — ischemic CN III palsy (from microvascular disease in diabetes or hypertension) spares the pupil; pupil involvement points to compression, not ischemia.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man presents with episodes of severe right periorbital pain lasting 30-45 minutes, occurring 4-5 times per day for the past 3 weeks. He reports right-sided lacrimation and nasal congestion during episodes and says he is 'extremely agitated' during attacks. His wife notes he paces during the episodes. He reports the attacks began suddenly 3 weeks ago and he has been headache-free for several years. Which primary headache disorder best fits this presentation?

options.A: Migraine with autonomic features
options.B: Trigeminal neuralgia
options.C: Cluster headache
options.D: Hemicrania continua

questions.rationale: Cluster headache is characterized by: unilateral severe periorbital pain lasting 15-180 minutes, occurring 1-8 times per day in cluster periods (weeks to months), ipsilateral cranial autonomic features (lacrimation, rhinorrhea, ptosis, miosis), and the pathognomonic agitation and restlessness during attacks. The cyclical pattern (cluster period) and the agitation distinguish this from migraine. Option A is incorrect: migraine autonomic features (migraine with cranial autonomic symptoms) also occur but migraine patients characteristically prefer to lie still in a dark quiet room — the agitation/pacing is pathognomonic for cluster headache. Option B is incorrect: trigeminal neuralgia presents with brief (seconds) electric shock-like facial pain triggered by light touch, chewing, or cold — not autonomic features, not periorbital, not minutes-long episodes. Option D is incorrect: hemicrania continua is a continuous unilateral headache with fluctuating severity and cranial autonomic features that is indomethacin-responsive — it lacks the episodic cluster pattern and does not remit spontaneously.

questions.stem: A 41-year-old man with confirmed cluster headache presents at the start of a new cluster period. He is requesting his usual sumatriptan 50mg oral tablets. What is the most appropriate acute treatment for a cluster headache attack?

options.A: Sumatriptan 50mg oral tablets as prescribed
options.B: High-flow oxygen at 100% via non-rebreather mask for 15 minutes, AND sumatriptan 6mg subcutaneous
options.C: Verapamil 240mg daily for prophylaxis; no acute rescue needed once preventive is on board
options.D: Ergotamine 2mg sublingual at onset of each attack

questions.rationale: Cluster headache acute treatment requires fast-acting routes — the pain peaks rapidly in minutes, making oral medications ineffective (they absorb too slowly to achieve therapeutic levels before the attack peaks). First-line acute cluster treatment: high-flow oxygen 100% via non-rebreather mask at 12-15 L/min for 15 minutes (highly effective, no systemic side effects) AND/OR subcutaneous sumatriptan 6mg (fastest onset of all triptan formulations, most evidence). Option A is incorrect: oral sumatriptan absorbs too slowly for cluster headache — by the time it reaches therapeutic levels, the attack may be over; it is specifically not recommended as monotherapy for acute cluster attacks. Option C is incorrect: verapamil is the preventive (prophylactic) treatment for cluster — it does not treat acute attacks; patients still need effective acute rescue therapy. Option D is incorrect: ergotamines are largely obsolete, have significant CV contraindications, and are not first-line cluster treatment per current guidelines.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old woman with a 10-year history of migraine presents reporting her headaches have become daily over the past 4 months. She uses sumatriptan 6 days per week to manage. She denies any recent head trauma or new neurological symptoms. On review, her current medication list shows sumatriptan, ibuprofen 800mg BID, and caffeine tablets daily. What is the most likely diagnosis and the cornerstone of management?

options.A: Chronic migraine requiring biologic (CGRP antibody) initiation; continue current acute medications
options.B: Medication overuse headache (MOH); the cornerstone of management is analgesic withdrawal
options.C: New daily persistent headache; order MRI brain with and without contrast
options.D: Tension-type headache transformation; switch ibuprofen to naproxen sodium

questions.rationale: Medication overuse headache (MOH) develops when headache-specific medications (triptans ≥10 days/month) or analgesics/NSAIDs (≥15 days/month) are used regularly — causing rebound headache and a transformation to daily or near-daily headache. Sumatriptan use 6 days per week far exceeds the threshold. The cornerstone of management is discontinuing the overused medication(s). Patients must be counseled that withdrawal will transiently worsen headache before improving. Option A is incorrect: CGRP monoclonal antibodies are appropriate for episodic or chronic migraine prevention, but they are not the cornerstone of MOH management — withdrawal of overused medication is; adding preventive therapy without withdrawal will not resolve MOH. Option C is incorrect: new daily persistent headache is a distinct entity with headache continuous from onset (not progressive transformation from episodic); neuroimaging is not required when the presentation fits MOH. Option D is incorrect: switching NSAIDs does not address the fundamental problem — the frequency of analgesic use, not the specific agent, is causing the MOH.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with HIV (CD4 count 42) presents with 2 weeks of progressive headache, mild confusion, and low-grade fever. His temperature is 38.1°C. There is no focal neurological deficit and no papilledema. Which is the most appropriate initial diagnostic and management sequence?

options.A: Perform lumbar puncture immediately; send CSF for bacterial culture, cryptococcal antigen, and cell count
options.B: Obtain CT head first to exclude mass lesion, then proceed with LP; send cryptococcal antigen serum simultaneously
options.C: Start empiric ceftriaxone for bacterial meningitis and delay LP for 72 hours
options.D: Start empiric fluconazole for cryptococcal meningitis based on CD4 count and symptom pattern

questions.rationale: In an immunocompromised patient with CD4 <50, cryptococcal meningitis is the most likely diagnosis and must be evaluated with LP and CSF cryptococcal antigen. However, with altered mentation in an immunocompromised host, a mass lesion (CNS toxoplasmosis, lymphoma) must be excluded by CT head before LP to rule out herniation risk. CT first, then LP is the correct sequence, with serum cryptococcal antigen sent simultaneously (highly sensitive even before LP). Option A is incorrect: LP before CT is unsafe in an immunocompromised patient with cognitive changes — toxoplasma or CNS lymphoma can cause elevated ICP and herniation risk even without papilledema. Option C is incorrect: empiric ceftriaxone without LP is appropriate for bacterial meningitis in classic presentations, but this patient's subacute course, low CD4, and HIV status makes cryptococcal meningitis the priority; fluconazole would be the empiric choice, not ceftriaxone alone, and LP is essential for diagnosis and to measure opening pressure. Option D is incorrect: starting fluconazole without LP misses the opening pressure measurement and culture, which are critical in cryptococcal meningitis — amphotericin B plus flucytosine is first-line induction therapy (not fluconazole) for CNS cryptococcosis; empiric treatment without LP is incomplete management.

questions.stem: A 17-year-old male basketball player collapsed during a game and briefly lost consciousness. Bystanders performed CPR until he regained consciousness spontaneously 45 seconds later. He felt dizzy and weak for 10 minutes after. He denies prodrome before collapse. His father died suddenly at age 42 from 'a heart problem.' ECG shows increased QRS voltage and deep Q waves in lateral leads. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Reassure the family that vasovagal syncope is common in adolescents; follow up in 2 weeks
options.B: Obtain cardiac MRI and refer to cardiology urgently; restrict from sports pending evaluation
options.C: Prescribe beta-blockers empirically and allow return to sports with 48-hour monitoring
options.D: Perform tilt-table testing at next available appointment to confirm vasovagal etiology

questions.rationale: Exertional syncope in a young athlete with a family history of sudden cardiac death and ECG findings consistent with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (increased QRS voltage, deep Q waves lateral leads) is a cardiac emergency until proven otherwise. HOCM is the leading cause of sudden cardiac death in young athletes in the US. He must be immediately restricted from competitive sports and evaluated urgently with echocardiogram and cardiology referral. Option A is incorrect: reassurance for vasovagal is appropriate for classic presentations — exertional syncope without prodrome in a young athlete with a concerning family history and ECG cannot be attributed to vasovagal without cardiac workup. Option C is incorrect: prescribing beta-blockers without a confirmed diagnosis and allowing return to sports before workup is dangerous — premature return to exertion risks sudden cardiac death. Option D is incorrect: tilt-table testing evaluates vasovagal or orthostatic syncope — it has no diagnostic role for suspected HOCM and is not the appropriate study here.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents after a witnessed syncope episode while gardening. His wife reports he looked pale before the event, then went limp for about 20 seconds and recovered fully within 2 minutes. He reports he had been standing in the heat for an hour and had not drunk water. Current ECG is normal. BP sitting 132/82, standing 130/80. Past medical history: hypertension on amlodipine. Which diagnosis is most consistent with this presentation?

options.A: Cardiac arrhythmia requiring urgent Holter monitoring
options.B: Vasovagal syncope with situational trigger
options.C: Orthostatic hypotension secondary to amlodipine
options.D: Seizure with tonic-clonic activity misidentified as syncope

questions.rationale: Vasovagal syncope has three key features met here: (1) prodrome (pallor, brief warning), (2) situational trigger (prolonged standing in heat, dehydration), and (3) brief LOC with rapid complete recovery. Normal ECG and normal orthostatic vitals remove orthostatic hypotension and cardiac arrhythmia from consideration. Option A is incorrect: cardiac arrhythmia-related syncope typically has no prodrome, occurs without clear trigger, and may occur while seated or supine — the classic vasovagal pattern with a clear trigger and prodrome argues strongly against arrhythmia; however, a normal ECG in the office does not exclude all arrhythmias. Option C is incorrect: orthostatic hypotension requires a ≥20 mmHg systolic drop on standing — here orthostatic vitals are normal; furthermore, amlodipine causes peripheral vasodilation but is not a major orthostatic agent. Option D is incorrect: seizure should be suspected with prolonged loss of consciousness, post-ictal confusion, lateral tongue biting, or incontinence — none of these are present; the 20-second LOC with immediate recovery is more consistent with syncope.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman presents after a syncope episode. Her ECG shows a QTc of 508 ms. She recently started moxifloxacin and haloperidol. She has no structural heart disease on prior echocardiogram. Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Continue current medications; QTc of 508 ms is acceptable in elderly women
options.B: Refer to cardiology at next available appointment for outpatient QTc management
options.C: Discontinue QT-prolonging medications and transfer to ED for monitoring and cardiac event telemetry
options.D: Prescribe potassium supplementation and recheck QTc in 1 week

questions.rationale: QTc 508 ms is critically prolonged (normal female <470 ms) and, combined with syncope, indicates high risk for torsades de pointes (TdP) — a potentially fatal polymorphic ventricular tachycardia. Moxifloxacin and haloperidol are both QT-prolonging agents, and their combination significantly increases TdP risk. This is not an outpatient situation — the patient requires QT-prolonging drug discontinuation, telemetry monitoring, electrolyte optimization, and emergent evaluation. Option A is incorrect: QTc 508 ms with syncope is not acceptable in any age group — TdP risk at this QTc is substantial, particularly with syncope already occurring. Option B is incorrect: outpatient next-available cardiology appointment is inappropriate for a patient with syncope and QTc 508 ms — this requires emergency-level monitoring. Option D is incorrect: while electrolyte correction is important in QT management, it is not sufficient without discontinuing the causative agents and monitoring — hypomagnesemia can be a contributing factor but this patient's primary issue is drug-induced QT prolongation.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents after a witnessed event during which she suddenly went limp while sitting at her desk, eyes rolling back briefly. A coworker reports she made rhythmic leg movements for about 8 seconds and then was confused for 15 minutes afterward before returning to her baseline. She has no prior history of seizure or syncope. Which feature most strongly supports seizure over syncope in this presentation?

options.A: Brief rhythmic movements lasting 8 seconds during the LOC
options.B: Eyes rolling back during the event
options.C: Sudden onset without warning while seated
options.D: Confusion lasting 15 minutes after regaining consciousness

questions.rationale: Post-ictal confusion lasting 15 minutes after regaining consciousness is the most discriminating feature for seizure versus syncope. Syncope-related anoxic myoclonus (brief jerking during oxygen deprivation) is common and does NOT indicate epilepsy — it resolves when consciousness returns. Confusion after syncope is brief (<1 minute). Prolonged post-ictal confusion (minutes to tens of minutes) strongly suggests a generalized seizure. Option A is incorrect: brief rhythmic movements during LOC are a normal manifestation of anoxic myoclonus in syncope — they should not be interpreted as convulsive seizure activity when the duration is very short (8 seconds); however, prolonged tonic-clonic activity >1 minute is more concerning. Option B is incorrect: eye deviation or rolling can occur in both syncope and seizure. Option C is incorrect: sudden onset without warning while seated is a feature of cardiac syncope but is non-specific — arrhythmia-related syncope also lacks prodrome; it does not distinguish seizure from cardiac syncope.

questions.stem: An 81-year-old man with hypertension and BPH presents after two syncopal episodes in the past 3 months. Both occurred after meals. BP sitting: 148/84, standing at 1 minute: 118/68, standing at 3 minutes: 104/62. Current medications: tamsulosin 0.4mg daily, metoprolol 50mg BID, amlodipine 10mg daily. What is the most appropriate intervention?

options.A: Increase metoprolol to reduce heart rate and prevent compensatory tachycardia
options.B: Start fludrocortisone 0.1mg daily immediately for all-cause orthostatic syncope
options.C: Discontinue tamsulosin or reduce antihypertensive regimen; counsel on meal-related hypotension
options.D: Refer to cardiology for loop recorder implantation to monitor for arrhythmia

questions.rationale: This patient has progressive orthostatic hypotension (BP drops 44 mmHg systolic at 3 minutes standing) that is worse postprandially — consistent with medication-induced orthostasis from the combination of tamsulosin (alpha-blocker) plus amlodipine (calcium channel blocker) plus metoprolol (beta-blocker reduces compensatory tachycardia). The first intervention for drug-induced orthostatic hypotension is deprescribing or reducing the causative agents, not adding another medication. Option A is incorrect: increasing metoprolol would worsen orthostatic hypotension by further blunting the compensatory tachycardia that partially maintains blood pressure on standing. Option B is incorrect: fludrocortisone may be appropriate for refractory neurogenic orthostasis, but adding a mineralocorticoid before addressing the drug burden would expose this patient to sodium retention, hypokalemia, and potentially worsening hypertension. Option D is incorrect: cardiac arrhythmia is not the likely mechanism here — the orthostatic BP response clearly identifies the etiology; loop recorder implantation would be appropriate if arrhythmia were the primary concern after medication optimization fails.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old woman with HFrEF (EF 30%) and T2DM presents after an episode of loss of consciousness while sitting. She denies prodrome. ECG shows sinus rhythm with LBBB. Troponin is negative. BP 110/70. Which syncope evaluation is most appropriate for this patient?

options.A: Diagnose vasovagal syncope based on normal troponin and discharge with follow-up in 2 weeks
options.B: Transfer to ED for hospital-level monitoring, cardiology evaluation, and advanced arrhythmia workup
options.C: Schedule 48-hour Holter monitor as outpatient and follow up in 3-4 days
options.D: Perform tilt-table test to rule out vasovagal mechanism before cardiac workup

questions.rationale: This patient meets multiple high-risk syncope criteria: structural heart disease (HFrEF with EF 30%), abnormal ECG (LBBB — highest-risk ECG finding in syncope), and syncope without prodrome while seated. The San Francisco Syncope Rule identifies HF history alone as a high-risk criterion. These factors together predict high 30-day serious outcome risk (cardiac arrhythmia, sudden death). This patient requires hospital admission for telemetry monitoring and cardiology evaluation, not outpatient workup. Option A is incorrect: negative troponin does not reassure against arrhythmic syncope — troponin excludes ACS, not VT or AV block. Option C is incorrect: outpatient Holter monitoring is appropriate for low-risk or intermediate-risk patients — this patient with HFrEF and LBBB-associated syncope is high-risk and cannot safely wait days for monitoring. Option D is incorrect: tilt-table testing evaluates vasovagal/orthostatic mechanisms — it has no diagnostic utility in a patient with structural heart disease and high-risk ECG findings where arrhythmia is the priority.

questions.stem: A 54-year-old man presents to urgent care with BP 196/114, headache, and anxiety. He is alert and oriented. Neurological exam is normal. Fundoscopy shows no papilledema. He has been non-adherent to his medications. His ECG is normal and creatinine is at his baseline. What is the most appropriate management?

options.A: Transfer to the emergency department for IV antihypertensive infusion
options.B: Administer sublingual nifedipine 10mg for rapid BP reduction
options.C: Have him rest in a quiet room for 20 minutes, restart his oral medications, and arrange 24-48h follow-up
options.D: Start IV labetalol in clinic to bring BP below 160/100 within 1 hour

questions.rationale: This is hypertensive urgency — severely elevated BP without acute end-organ damage (normal neuro exam, no papilledema, normal ECG, baseline creatinine). Hypertensive urgency does not require emergency hospitalization or IV therapy. The correct management is: 20-30 minutes of rest (often drops BP significantly), restart or adjust oral antihypertensives, and arrange close 24-48h follow-up. Option A is incorrect: ED transfer is appropriate for hypertensive emergency (end-organ damage), not urgency. Option B is incorrect: sublingual nifedipine is specifically contraindicated — it causes rapid, unpredictable, uncontrolled BP reduction that can precipitate ischemic stroke, MI, and death; this is a recognized patient safety error. Option D is incorrect: IV labetalol is reserved for hypertensive emergency with target organ damage; administering IV antihypertensives in clinic for urgency without monitoring capability is inappropriate and potentially dangerous.

questions.stem: A 59-year-old woman presents with sudden onset severe chest and back pain radiating to the scapulae. BP is 188/104 in the right arm and 148/80 in the left arm. Heart rate is 108. She appears diaphoretic. Chest X-ray shows widened mediastinum. What is the most critical immediate step?

options.A: Administer aspirin 325mg and call cardiology for STEMI protocol activation
options.B: Start IV esmolol or labetalol to reduce HR and BP; call 911 for immediate transfer
options.C: Start IV nitroprusside to rapidly reduce BP below 120/80
options.D: Perform two-view chest X-ray and await radiologist confirmation before initiating treatment

questions.rationale: This is acute aortic dissection: tearing scapular pain, inter-arm BP differential >40 mmHg systolic, widened mediastinum on CXR, and tachycardia. The immediate treatment goal is to reduce aortic wall shear stress: decrease HR to <60 bpm first, then reduce SBP to 100-120 mmHg. A beta-blocker (esmolol or labetalol) must be started FIRST — vasodilators alone cause reflex tachycardia, which INCREASES aortic wall shear force and can propagate the dissection. Immediate surgical consultation and transfer are life-saving. Option A is incorrect: aspirin is for ACS — aortic dissection is not a thrombotic event and aspirin could worsen bleeding into the dissection; activating STEMI protocol delays the correct treatment. Option C is incorrect: nitroprusside (a vasodilator) alone causes reflex tachycardia — it should only be used AFTER the heart rate has been adequately reduced with a beta-blocker; starting nitroprusside alone worsens dissection. Option D is incorrect: treatment must not be delayed for radiologist confirmation when the clinical diagnosis is clear — await the call to 911 and begin beta-blocker therapy simultaneously.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman at 36 weeks gestation is brought to a clinic visit by her partner. BP is 176/110 (confirmed twice). She reports a severe frontal headache and visual changes ('sparkles') for 3 hours. She has 3+ proteinuria. She denies contractions. What is the most appropriate action?

options.A: Start oral labetalol and admit for fetal monitoring; check labs in the morning
options.B: Administer magnesium sulfate, call 911 for immediate hospital transfer, and initiate antihypertensive treatment
options.C: Administer sublingual nifedipine for rapid BP reduction and observe for 2 hours in clinic
options.D: Consult nephrology for presumed pregnancy-associated hypertension with proteinuria

questions.rationale: Severe-range BP (≥160/110) with headache and visual symptoms in a woman at 36 weeks gestation is preeclampsia with severe features — approaching eclampsia. Magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis must be given IMMEDIATELY (not waiting for a seizure to occur). Antihypertensive treatment (IV labetalol or hydralazine) must be initiated, and the patient requires immediate transfer to an obstetric facility — delivery is the definitive treatment. Option A is incorrect: oral labetalol alone in a clinic without magnesium sulfate is dangerously inadequate for BP 176/110 with prodromal neurological symptoms; the patient is on the verge of eclampsia. Option C is incorrect: sublingual nifedipine is contraindicated in general and even more dangerous in pregnancy; a 2-hour observation in clinic is unsafe for a patient on the verge of seizure. Option D is incorrect: nephrology consultation is not the priority — this is an obstetric emergency requiring immediate obstetric hospital transfer, not ambulatory specialist consultation.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with a 20-year history of hypertension presents with BP 204/118 after 3 days without his medications. He had accidentally run out. He is alert, oriented, and has no headache, chest pain, or vision change. Creatinine is at his baseline. Which is the most appropriate initial management approach?

options.A: Administer IV hydralazine 10mg to rapidly lower BP within 1 hour
options.B: Restart his prescribed oral antihypertensives and follow up in 24-48 hours
options.C: Admit to telemetry for 24-hour monitoring given BP >200 systolic
options.D: Start oral nifedipine extended-release 30mg and decrease dose of his beta-blocker

questions.rationale: This is hypertensive urgency in a chronically hypertensive patient who ran out of his medications — the BP is elevated due to abrupt discontinuation, not a new problem. He has no end-organ damage. The correct approach is to restart his prescribed medications, observe him for 20-30 minutes to confirm stability, and arrange 24-48h follow-up. In chronic hypertension, the brain's autoregulatory curve is shifted — rapid lowering to normal BP causes cerebral ischemia in a patient whose brain has adapted to higher pressure. Option A is incorrect: IV hydralazine is reserved for hypertensive emergency with end-organ damage; administering IV medications for urgency risks excessive rapid lowering and is inappropriate for outpatient management. Option C is incorrect: hospital admission for telemetry is not indicated without end-organ damage; BP elevation alone (urgency) does not require inpatient management. Option D is incorrect: changing his medication regimen during an acute event without knowing his stable regimen is inappropriate — the correct action is to restart what was working, not empirically restructure therapy.

questions.stem: A 51-year-old woman with BP on three maximal-dose medications (lisinopril 40mg, amlodipine 10mg, and hydrochlorothiazide 25mg) still has BP averaging 158/96 at home. She is adherent to medications. She denies symptoms of secondary hypertension. Serum potassium is 3.4 mEq/L, sodium normal, creatinine normal. Which is the most appropriate fourth agent?

options.A: Metoprolol succinate 25mg daily
options.B: Clonidine 0.1mg BID
options.C: Spironolactone 25-50mg daily
options.D: Hydralazine 25mg TID

questions.rationale: Spironolactone is the evidence-based fourth agent for resistant hypertension — the PATHWAY-2 trial demonstrated its superiority over other agents as add-on therapy. Hypokalemia (K+ 3.4) in a patient on a thiazide diuretic suggests primary hyperaldosteronism, which is highly prevalent in resistant hypertension. Spironolactone directly antagonizes aldosterone, addresses the likely underlying aldosteronism, and raises potassium. Option A is incorrect: adding a second beta-blocker (metoprolol) to a patient already on ACEi, CCB, and thiazide has limited evidence as fourth agent; beta-blockers are less effective for resistant hypertension than spironolactone. Option B is incorrect: clonidine is a centrally acting alpha-agonist with significant side effects (sedation, rebound hypertension on discontinuation) and is not recommended as fourth-line agent in modern resistant HTN management. Option D is incorrect: hydralazine causes reflex tachycardia and requires TID dosing, making it difficult to use without a concurrent beta-blocker; it is not a preferred fourth agent for resistant hypertension.

questions.stem: An 84-year-old woman has been on amlodipine 10mg, lisinopril 20mg, and furosemide 20mg. Her home BPs average 142/78. At today's visit, BP sitting is 136/72; on standing, BP drops to 108/64 and she reports lightheadedness. She had a fall 3 months ago. How should her antihypertensive regimen be modified?

options.A: Continue current regimen; target BP <130/80 per ACC/AHA guidelines for all adults
options.B: Add midodrine 5mg BID to address orthostatic hypotension
options.C: Reduce or discontinue one antihypertensive agent, prioritizing removal of furosemide if no clear volume overload indication
options.D: Increase furosemide to 40mg to reduce volume and peripheral vascular resistance

questions.rationale: This 84-year-old has clinical orthostatic hypotension (systolic drop >20 mmHg) with a history of falls — antihypertensive-induced orthostasis is her primary fall risk. Her sitting BP of 136/72 is reasonable for an 84-year-old frail patient; ACC/AHA targets of <130/80 apply to healthy non-frail older adults but should be relaxed in frail patients with orthostasis. Furosemide should be deprescribed first if there is no active heart failure or volume overload indication — it causes volume depletion that worsens orthostasis. Option A is incorrect: rigidly applying <130/80 to a frail 84-year-old with orthostatic hypotension and a fall history is likely to cause more harm from falls and cerebral hypoperfusion than from slightly higher BP. Option B is incorrect: midodrine raises BP via vasoconstriction but may worsen supine hypertension in elderly patients — the correct first step is to reduce the antihypertensive burden, not to add a vasoconstrictor. Option D is incorrect: increasing furosemide would worsen volume depletion and dramatically worsen orthostatic hypotension — this is the opposite of what is needed.

questions.stem: A 53-year-old man presents with 4 weeks of difficulty swallowing solids that has now progressed to liquids. He has lost 11 pounds in the past 6 weeks without trying. He has a 25 pack-year smoking history and drinks 2-3 glasses of wine nightly. He was seen 6 months ago and had a 'normal' upper endoscopy for GERD. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Increase his PPI to twice daily and trial for 8 weeks before further workup
options.B: Order barium swallow to characterize swallowing dysfunction before endoscopy
options.C: Refer urgently for upper endoscopy (EGD) within 2 weeks
options.D: Reassure and recheck BMI in 4 weeks given recent normal endoscopy

questions.rationale: Progressive dysphagia (solids to liquids), unintentional weight loss, and multiple risk factors (smoking, alcohol) are esophageal cancer alarm features requiring urgent endoscopic evaluation, not empiric trial of PPI or reassurance. A 'normal' endoscopy 6 months ago does not protect against interval malignancy, especially with new obstructive symptoms. Upper endoscopy (EGD) is the definitive test for esophageal cancer and should be arranged within 2 weeks per NICE NG12 guidelines. Option A is incorrect: empiric PPI for progressive dysphagia with weight loss represents dangerous delay — GERD does not progress to dysphagia with liquids within weeks; this pattern is cancer until proven otherwise. Option B is incorrect: barium swallow is an indirect imaging test and would delay the definitive diagnosis achievable by EGD with biopsy; it is not the appropriate first step for a mass lesion workup. Option D is incorrect: reassurance based on a prior normal endoscopy is clinically negligent in the setting of new alarm symptoms; adenocarcinoma can develop rapidly in Barrett's esophagus.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man presents for a well visit. He mentions he noticed blood in his urine 'once, about 10 days ago' — described as pink-tinged voiding. It has not recurred. He denies pain, UTI symptoms, or dysuria. He is a 35 pack-year smoker. Current urinalysis today is completely normal. What is the most appropriate management?

options.A: Reassure and monitor; a single episode with a normal UA today is likely benign
options.B: Treat for presumed UTI with nitrofurantoin and recheck UA in 2 weeks
options.C: Order CT urogram and refer to urology for cystoscopy
options.D: Repeat UA in 4 weeks; if blood persists, refer to urology

questions.rationale: A single episode of gross (visible) hematuria in a male ≥35 with a significant smoking history mandates full urological evaluation — CT urogram AND cystoscopy — regardless of a current normal UA. Bladder cancer and renal cell carcinoma are the primary concerns. Urothelial bleeding is intermittent, and a normal UA on the day of visit does not exclude urological malignancy. Delay for 'watchful waiting' significantly worsens outcomes in bladder cancer, which is often diagnosed late. Option A is incorrect: reassurance based on a single episode and a normal follow-up UA is an unacceptable miss for potentially curable bladder cancer — intermittent bleeding is a feature of this malignancy, not evidence of its absence. Option B is incorrect: there are no UTI symptoms; treating without UA positivity for infection is inappropriate stewardship and delays cancer diagnosis. Option D is incorrect: waiting 4 weeks before referral introduces dangerous delay in a patient with a smoking history and a cancer-risk hematuria presentation; every episode of gross hematuria in this demographic needs full workup.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman presents with 3 weeks of drenching night sweats, a 15-pound weight loss, and fatigue. She notices a firm non-tender lump above her left collarbone. She denies fever. CBC shows mild normocytic anemia, LDH 340 U/L (elevated). What is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Order an infectious disease workup for mycobacterium and start empiric antifungals
options.B: Reassure and recheck CBC in 4 weeks; B-symptoms alone are non-specific
options.C: Order urgent CT of chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast and refer to hematology-oncology
options.D: Aspirate the supraclavicular lymph node for culture and sensitivity

questions.rationale: B-symptoms (drenching night sweats, weight loss), firm supraclavicular lymphadenopathy (Virchow's region for left neck — drainage from mediastinum, thorax, and upper abdomen), elevated LDH, and anemia in a 62-year-old constitute a lymphoma pattern requiring urgent oncological evaluation. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis is the appropriate first staging workup. Hematology-oncology referral for lymph node excisional biopsy is the standard diagnostic approach.
