Option A is incorrect: this is not a mycobacterial or fungal pattern — the combination of B-symptoms, supraclavicular adenopathy, and elevated LDH is a malignant hematological constellation; antifungal therapy delays cancer diagnosis. Option B is incorrect: B-symptoms are not non-specific in this context — they are a WHO staging criteria for lymphoma and warrant urgent evaluation. Option D is incorrect: aspiration of supraclavicular lymph nodes (FNA) is inadequate for lymphoma diagnosis — lymphoma requires architecture analysis, which necessitates excisional biopsy; aspirating the node delays correct diagnostic tissue sampling.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old postmenopausal woman notices a new 1.5 cm firm mass in her right breast at her annual exam. She had a screening bilateral mammogram 8 months ago read as BI-RADS 1 (normal). She has no family history of breast cancer. What is the most appropriate management?

options.A: Reassure and repeat screening mammogram at her next annual appointment in 4 months
options.B: Order a diagnostic mammogram and breast ultrasound immediately
options.C: Refer to a breast surgeon without imaging since the clinical exam is suspicious
options.D: Schedule a repeat clinical breast exam in 3 months to monitor for size change

questions.rationale: A new palpable breast mass in a postmenopausal woman requires immediate imaging regardless of a recent normal screening mammogram. Palpable breast masses need diagnostic mammogram (not screening) and ultrasound — diagnostic imaging includes additional targeted views and is interpreted differently than screening. A normal screening mammogram does not clear a new palpable mass, as mammography has 10-15% false negative rate for palpable lesions. Option A is incorrect: repeating a screening mammogram in 4 months is inadequate — the screening is already 8 months old, and the mass is new and palpable; screening mammogram is not the same as diagnostic evaluation. Option C is incorrect: surgical referral without imaging is premature — imaging first characterizes the lesion and guides biopsy decision; biopsy without imaging is not standard of care. Option D is incorrect: a 3-month delay to 'monitor' a new firm breast mass in a postmenopausal woman is a clinical error — postmenopausal breast tissue does not normally develop benign cysts; all palpable masses require evaluation.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with a 25 pack-year history quit smoking 12 years ago. His coworker was just diagnosed with lung cancer and the patient asks if he qualifies for lung cancer screening. His most recent visit was 14 months ago and no screening was ordered. Which statement accurately applies the USPSTF 2021 lung cancer screening criteria?

options.A: He does not qualify; he must currently smoke to be eligible for LDCT screening
options.B: He qualifies; USPSTF recommends annual LDCT for adults 50-80 with ≥20 pack-years who currently smoke or quit within 15 years
options.C: He qualifies only if he also has a first-degree relative with lung cancer
options.D: He does not qualify; annual chest X-ray is the recommended screening modality for former smokers

questions.rationale: The USPSTF 2021 criteria for annual low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening: adults age 50-80, ≥20 pack-year smoking history, and currently smoking OR quit within the past 15 years. This patient: age 52 (qualifies), 25 pack-years (qualifies), quit 12 years ago (within 15 years — qualifies). He should have been offered LDCT at his visit 14 months ago. Option A is incorrect: the 2021 guideline update specifically expanded eligibility to include former smokers who quit within 15 years — former smoking status within this window qualifies. Option C is incorrect: family history is not a USPSTF criterion for LDCT eligibility — only age, pack-year history, and current vs recent smoking status determine eligibility. Option D is incorrect: chest X-ray has no proven mortality benefit for lung cancer screening and is not recommended — LDCT is the only evidence-based screening modality with demonstrated mortality reduction (NLST trial).

questions.stem: A patient presents requesting to start PrEP. He identifies as a gay man who has had condomless anal sex with multiple partners in the past 3 months. His last HIV test was 6 months ago and was negative. What is the minimum requirement before initiating TDF/FTC (Truvada) today?

options.A: Provide a new HIV test order but initiate PrEP based on the 6-month-old negative result
options.B: Obtain a new HIV test within 7 days prior to PrEP initiation and confirm negative status
options.C: Obtain a new HIV test and wait 30 days for results before prescribing
options.D: Initiate PrEP immediately; HIV testing can be ordered at the first follow-up visit

questions.rationale: CDC PrEP guidelines require confirmed HIV-negative status within 7 days prior to initiating PrEP. A 6-month-old negative test is insufficient — the patient may have acquired HIV during the interval, and starting PrEP with active undiagnosed HIV selects for resistance to the tenofovir/emtricitabine backbone. A 4th-generation HIV antigen/antibody test (or HIV RNA) within 7 days is required. Option A is incorrect: the 6-month-old test exceeds the allowable window; the patient must have recent testing. Option C is incorrect: HIV test results from 4th-generation testing are available within hours to days — waiting 30 days is not clinically necessary and delays access to prevention. Option D is incorrect: PrEP must NEVER be initiated without confirming HIV-negative status — this is the primary patient safety requirement of PrEP prescribing.

questions.stem: A transgender woman on gender-affirming estrogen therapy is requesting PrEP. She has receptive anal sex with male partners. She has been inconsistent with daily pill-taking in the past. She has no chronic kidney disease. Which PrEP regimen is most appropriate to offer?

options.A: TAF/FTC (Descovy) — preferred for transgender women with better renal and bone safety
options.B: TDF/FTC (Truvada) — the only approved regimen for transgender women
options.C: Both Truvada and Descovy plus injectable cabotegravir should be discussed; injectable preferred for adherence
options.D: TDF/FTC oral only; injectable PrEP is not approved for transgender women

questions.rationale: For a transgender woman with receptive anal sex and documented difficulty with daily adherence, presenting all appropriate options — including injectable cabotegravir (Apretude) — is the correct approach. Cabotegravir long-acting injectable (every 2 months) was specifically studied in transgender women in HPTN 083 and showed superiority to daily oral TDF/FTC. Descovy (TAF/FTC) is approved for MSM and transgender women — it is appropriate. Truvada is also appropriate. For a patient with adherence challenges, discussing injectable PrEP is particularly important. Option A is incorrect: while Descovy is appropriate, limiting the option to Descovy only excludes the injectable option and Truvada — an incomplete response. Option B is incorrect: Truvada is not 'the only approved regimen'; Descovy and injectable cabotegravir are both approved for transgender women. Option D is incorrect: injectable cabotegravir (Apretude) IS approved for transgender women — this was a primary enrollment group in the pivotal HPTN 083 trial.

questions.stem: A cisgender woman and her male partner (HIV-positive, undetectable viral load) have decided to try to conceive. She does not have HIV. She requests PrEP. Which PrEP regimen is most appropriate for her as a woman who will have condomless vaginal sex?

options.A: TAF/FTC (Descovy) — preferred for all patients due to better renal safety
options.B: TDF/FTC (Truvada) — FDA-approved for vaginal exposure risk reduction in cisgender women
options.C: Injectable cabotegravir (Apretude) — more convenient and does not require daily pills
options.D: No PrEP needed; an undetectable viral load in her partner means U=U and transmission risk is effectively zero

questions.rationale: TDF/FTC (Truvada) is the only FDA-approved oral PrEP regimen for cisgender women with vaginal exposure risk. TAF/FTC (Descovy) was NOT studied in cisgender women for vaginal exposure and is NOT approved for this indication — a critical prescribing distinction. Injectable cabotegravir is FDA-approved for cisgender women (HPTN 084 enrolled cisgender women in Africa), so option C is partially correct but option B is the most straightforward evidence-based answer for oral PrEP. Option A is incorrect: Descovy is explicitly not approved for vaginal exposure in cisgender women — this is a patient safety error; prescribing it for this indication is off-label and potentially inadequate due to lower vaginal tissue drug concentrations. Option D is incorrect: U=U (Undetectable = Untransmittable) is scientifically robust for condomless sex with a virally suppressed partner, but this patient is asking for PrEP and has the right to make her own risk mitigation choices; some patients desire additional protection during conception attempts.

questions.stem: A gay man on TDF/FTC PrEP for 6 months presents for his routine 3-month visit. He reports he missed 3 doses this week due to travel. His HIV test today shows reactive 4th-generation result, confirmed reactive on repeat. He is completely asymptomatic. What is the most critical next step?

options.A: Recheck HIV test in 2 weeks to confirm; a single reactive result may be a false positive
options.B: Stop TDF/FTC immediately, order HIV RNA and resistance genotype, and refer urgently to HIV specialist
options.C: Add dolutegravir to the TDF/FTC and continue until resistance testing returns
options.D: Reassure the patient that he is protected while on PrEP and that seroconversion on PrEP is rare

questions.rationale: HIV seroconversion while on PrEP — confirmed reactive result — requires immediate action. TDF/FTC must be stopped immediately: continuing it as monotherapy in active HIV infection selects for tenofovir and emtricitabine resistance mutations (K65R and M184V), which would then compromise his future ART options. HIV RNA quantification confirms viremia, and resistance genotyping on the same sample guides ART selection. Urgent HIV specialist referral within days (not weeks) is needed. Option A is incorrect: a confirmed reactive result on repeat testing is not a laboratory false positive; delaying action risks resistance development while the patient is on inadequate therapy. Option C is incorrect: adding dolutegravir alone to TDF/FTC without resistance testing creates a partially suppressive regimen that may not achieve full viral suppression and still risks resistance; the full ART regimen must be designed by an HIV specialist after resistance results. Option D is incorrect: reassurance is inappropriate — seroconversion has occurred and the patient needs immediate HIV care.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old gay man on TDF/FTC PrEP asks how soon the medication protects against anal HIV acquisition. He started PrEP 5 days ago. What is the accurate counseling regarding time to protective drug levels?

options.A: Protection begins within 24-48 hours of starting TDF/FTC
options.B: Protection in rectal tissue is achieved after approximately 7 days of consistent daily dosing
options.C: Full protection requires 21 days of consistent dosing for all exposure routes
options.D: TDF/FTC provides no meaningful protection until 6 weeks of continuous use

questions.rationale: TDF/FTC achieves protective drug concentrations in rectal tissue after approximately 7 days of consistent daily dosing — this is based on pharmacokinetic studies showing rectal tissue saturation. This timeline is faster than vaginal tissue (21 days) due to differential drug concentration in mucosal tissues. The patient is at day 5 — he is not yet fully protected for rectal exposure and should be counseled to use condoms for the next 2 days. Option A is incorrect: 24-48 hours is insufficient for tissue saturation — blood levels rise quickly but intracellular (tissue) concentrations in rectal mucosa take 7 days. Option C is incorrect: 21 days is the timeline for vaginal tissue and for people who inject drugs, not rectal tissue — the distinction between 7 days (rectal) and 21 days (vaginal) is clinically important. Option D is incorrect: 6 weeks is far too long — this would be incorrect counseling that discourages appropriate use.

questions.stem: A new patient discloses that he has sex with men. To determine PrEP eligibility, the NP begins a sexual history. The patient identifies as heterosexual but discloses he has receptive anal sex with men while using methamphetamine at parties and does not disclose this to his wife. Which approach to sexual history demonstrates the highest quality inclusive practice?

options.A: Confirm the patient's heterosexual identity and proceed with a standard STI panel without further PrEP discussion
options.B: Ask directly about anatomy, sex acts, and partner anatomy without presupposing identity; complete a PrEP eligibility assessment based on behavioral risk
options.C: Inform the patient that he should disclose his behavior to his wife before initiating any sexual health services
options.D: Refrain from PrEP discussion since the patient identifies as heterosexual and the system is configured for MSM patients

questions.rationale: An inclusive, anatomy-based and behavior-based sexual history does not rely on identity labels to determine clinical risk. The patient has receptive anal sex with male partners — which is the highest-risk behavioral exposure for HIV — while disclosing a heterosexual identity. This is common, and identity-based screening misses this population. The NP should assess behavioral risk (sex acts, partner anatomy, condom use, substance use) and offer PrEP based on risk, not identity. Option A is incorrect: accepting the identity label and proceeding without PrEP discussion fails to assess the actual behavioral risk that makes this patient highly eligible for PrEP. Option C is incorrect: disclosure to his wife is a deeply personal decision and not within the NP's scope to require; confidentiality and non-judgment are foundational to inclusive sexual health care — creating barriers to care by moralizing will drive the patient away. Option D is incorrect: PrEP is not for 'MSM patients' — it is for any individual at substantial HIV risk regardless of identity; systems that restrict eligibility by identity label are clinically inappropriate.

questions.stem: A gay man on TDF/FTC PrEP presents for his 3-month monitoring visit. He is asymptomatic. Which of the following monitoring components is mandatory at this visit?

options.A: HIV test, creatinine, and comprehensive metabolic panel
options.B: HIV test only; all other labs can be checked at the 6-month visit
options.C: HIV test AND site-specific STI screening (rectal, urethral, pharyngeal NAAT)
options.D: HIV test, hepatitis B surface antigen, and hepatitis C antibody

questions.rationale: Every 3-month PrEP visit mandates: (1) HIV test — non-negotiable for both safety and continued eligibility; (2) site-specific STI screening — urethral, rectal, AND pharyngeal NAAT for gonorrhea and chlamydia. Site-specific screening (not just genital) triples STI detection rates in MSM. Creatinine/renal function is required every 6 months (not every 3 months). HBV and HCV are annual rechecks for MSM on PrEP. Option A is incorrect: CMP and creatinine are not required at every 3-month visit — they are checked at 6-month intervals for TDF/FTC; adding an unnecessary CMP at every visit is not guideline-recommended. Option B is incorrect: HIV test alone is insufficient — STI screening is equally essential and clinically mandated; missing STIs misses opportunities for treatment and partner notification. Option D is incorrect: HBV surface antigen is checked at baseline; repeat HBV surface antigen and HCV antibody are annual monitoring, not every-3-month requirements.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old woman completes the Timed Up and Go (TUG) test in your clinic. She rises from the chair, walks 10 feet, turns, and returns to sit in 14.5 seconds. How should you interpret this result?

options.A: Normal; TUG is abnormal only if it takes longer than 20 seconds
options.B: Mildly abnormal; follow up with gait assessment referral in 6 months
options.C: Abnormal; result ≥12 seconds indicates increased fall risk and warrants further assessment
options.D: The TUG cannot be interpreted without bone density data

questions.rationale: A TUG time of ≥12 seconds indicates increased fall risk in older adults. This patient completed the test in 14.5 seconds, placing her above the threshold. This result should trigger a comprehensive falls assessment: medication review, orthostatic BP measurement, vision evaluation, home safety review, and physical therapy referral for strength and balance training. Option A is incorrect: 20 seconds is not the cutoff — the validated fall risk threshold is ≥12 seconds; waiting for a 20-second TUG would miss the majority of at-risk older adults. Option B is incorrect: a positive TUG screening requires action now, not a 6-month follow-up; injury from a fall can occur before then. Option D is incorrect: the TUG is a purely functional assessment of gait, balance, and mobility — it is independent of bone density.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old man has undergone frailty screening using the Fried Frailty Criteria. He meets the following criteria: (1) unintentional weight loss of 5 kg in the past year, (2) self-reported exhaustion 3-4 days per week, and (3) slow gait speed on assessment. He has good grip strength and is physically active 3 days per week. What is his frailty classification and the most appropriate clinical response?

options.A: Robust (0 criteria); no intervention needed
options.B: Pre-frail (2 criteria); implement resistance exercise and nutritional optimization
options.C: Frail (3 criteria); initiate a comprehensive intervention program and refer to geriatrics
options.D: Frail (3 criteria); limit physical activity to prevent injury

questions.rationale: The patient meets 3 Fried Frailty criteria (weight loss, exhaustion, slow gait speed) — this classifies him as frail (≥3 criteria). Frailty requires a comprehensive intervention including resistance exercise, protein/nutritional assessment, review of all medications for contributors, and geriatrics consultation for complex management. Option A is incorrect: meeting 0 criteria is robust — this patient clearly meets 3. Option B is incorrect: 1-2 criteria = pre-frail; 3 or more = frail — this patient meets 3. The intervention intensity for frailty is greater than for pre-frailty. Option D is incorrect: limiting physical activity in frail elderly is harmful — progressive resistance exercise is the most evidence-based intervention for improving frailty; inactivity worsens muscle loss and functional decline.

questions.stem: A 77-year-old woman with insomnia has been taking zolpidem 10mg nightly for 4 years. She has had two falls in the past year, one resulting in a wrist fracture. She denies rebound insomnia when she has missed doses on rare occasions. Which management approach is most appropriate?

options.A: Continue zolpidem; it is safer than untreated insomnia in older adults
options.B: Switch to eszopiclone; it has a better fall risk profile than zolpidem
options.C: Discontinue zolpidem with a gradual taper; initiate cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT-I)
options.D: Add quetiapine 12.5mg nightly for sedation to allow zolpidem dose reduction

questions.rationale: Zolpidem (a Z-drug) is Beers Criteria in all older adults — it causes fall risk equivalent to benzodiazepines, impairs psychomotor function, causes next-day drowsiness, and contributes to cognitive impairment. With two falls and a fracture, this medication is causing documented harm. CBT-I (Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for Insomnia) is the first-line evidence-based treatment for chronic insomnia in all adults including the elderly — it outperforms medications in long-term outcomes. Gradual tapering prevents rebound insomnia. Option A is incorrect: continuing a Beers-listed medication causing documented falls is clinically unjustifiable — untreated insomnia is less acutely dangerous than falls and fractures in elderly. Option B is incorrect: eszopiclone is also a Z-drug with the same Beers listing and fall risk — substituting one Z-drug for another does not solve the problem. Option D is incorrect: adding quetiapine (an antipsychotic) for sleep is strongly discouraged in elderly patients — it carries Beers Criteria status for dementia patients, increases fall risk further, and adds anticholinergic and QT burden.

questions.stem: An 84-year-old man with moderate Alzheimer dementia, paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, and recurrent falls is being evaluated for anticoagulation. His CHA2DS2-VASc score is 4 (AF + hypertension + age). His daughter asks whether he should continue apixaban, which was started 6 months ago. He has had no bleeding events. Which approach best serves this patient?

options.A: Continue apixaban; stroke risk from AF outweighs fall risk in all patients with CHA2DS2-VASc ≥2
options.B: Discontinue apixaban; cognitive impairment plus fall risk make anticoagulation too dangerous
options.C: Conduct shared decision-making with patient (to extent possible) and family; present individualized stroke vs bleed risk data and patient's preferences and values
options.D: Switch from apixaban to aspirin 81mg for stroke prevention with lower bleed risk

questions.rationale: This is a genuine clinical dilemma — anticoagulation in a patient with high stroke risk AND high fall and bleed risk. Guidelines do not provide a definitive rule; shared decision-making with the patient (to the extent cognition allows) and family is the appropriate framework. Stroke risk data, intracranial hemorrhage risk data, fall risk factors, and the patient's and family's values regarding quality of life and acceptable outcomes must all be part of the discussion. Option A is incorrect: while stroke risk from AF is significant, dismissing fall and bleed risk is not person-centered — some patients in this situation have family-defined preferences that favor quality of life over stroke prevention. Option B is incorrect: blanket discontinuation based on cognitive impairment alone is not guideline-supported — cognition and fall risk are factors to weigh, not automatic contraindications; 'a patient must fall 295 times per year for falls to outweigh the stroke benefit of anticoagulation' is a commonly cited but context-dependent benchmark. Option D is incorrect: aspirin is not an alternative anticoagulant for AF stroke prevention — it does not reduce cardioembolic stroke and provides no meaningful benefit over no therapy for AF-related stroke.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old woman with T2DM, hypertension, and CKD has a serum creatinine of 1.4 mg/dL and weighs 52 kg. Her eGFR reported by the laboratory is 44 mL/min/1.73m². Her nephrologist has recommended starting sitagliptin. Using Cockcroft-Gault, her estimated CrCl calculates to 27 mL/min. What sitagliptin dosing is most appropriate?

options.A: Sitagliptin 100mg daily — use eGFR 44 which indicates Stage 3b CKD
options.B: Sitagliptin 25mg daily — Cockcroft-Gault CrCl <30 requires the lowest dose adjustment
options.C: Sitagliptin 50mg daily — CrCl 27 falls in the 30-50 range requiring moderate dose reduction
options.D: Avoid sitagliptin entirely; all DPP-4 inhibitors are contraindicated in CKD

questions.rationale: Drug dosing in the elderly must use Cockcroft-Gault CrCl, not CKD-EPI eGFR, because eGFR is BSA-normalized and overestimates renal function in low-muscle-mass elderly women. This patient's CrCl by Cockcroft-Gault is 27 mL/min (below 30). Sitagliptin dosing: CrCl ≥50 = 100mg; CrCl 30-49 = 50mg; CrCl <30 = 25mg. Using the eGFR of 44 would lead to a 50mg dose — which is actually an overdose relative to her true renal function. Option A is incorrect: using eGFR 44 leads to incorrect dosing — this patient's true renal function (CrCl 27) is substantially lower. Option C is incorrect: CrCl 27 is BELOW 30, not in the 30-50 range; 50mg would be an overdose for this renal function level. Option D is incorrect: DPP-4 inhibitors are not broadly contraindicated in CKD — they require dose adjustment. Linagliptin is the one DPP-4i that requires NO renal adjustment at any CKD stage.

questions.stem: An 81-year-old man visits his daughter who notices him becoming increasingly confused over 2 days. His medications include lisinopril, metoprolol, atorvastatin, tamsulosin, and diphenhydramine 50mg nightly for sleep (self-prescribed OTC). Vital signs are normal. UA is negative. Cognitive screen reveals new functional and executive deficits. What is the most likely contributing cause?

options.A: Early Alzheimer disease presenting with acute-on-chronic cognitive decline
options.B: Diphenhydramine-induced anticholinergic delirium
options.C: Urinary tract infection causing non-specific confusion
options.D: Metoprolol causing bradycardia-mediated cerebral hypoperfusion

questions.rationale: Diphenhydramine is a potent first-generation antihistamine with high anticholinergic CNS penetration. In elderly patients, anticholinergic agents cause acute confusion, delirium, urinary retention, constipation, and falls. This is the classic anticholinergic delirium pattern: subacute confusion over days, negative UA, normal vital signs, and an OTC agent often not disclosed without direct inquiry. Diphenhydramine should be stopped immediately. Option A is incorrect: Alzheimer's typically progresses over months to years; acute-onset confusion over 2 days without prior decline points to a reversible cause such as delirium. Option C is incorrect: UA is negative — treating for UTI without positive UA in an elderly confused patient is the classic overtreatment error; this patient's delirium is drug-induced. Option D is incorrect: metoprolol can cause fatigue but does not commonly cause acute cognitive confusion; heart rate is not reported as abnormal.

questions.stem: A 76-year-old man reports three falls in the past 6 months, one resulting in a rib contusion. His TUG is 15 seconds. He is sedentary. He has hypertension and osteoporosis. Current medications include amlodipine, lisinopril, atorvastatin, and furosemide. Home evaluation reveals poor lighting on stairs and a loose bath mat. Which multi-component fall prevention strategy is most evidence-based?

options.A: Prescribe hip protector pads and calcium plus vitamin D supplementation
options.B: Refer to physical therapy for progressive resistance and balance training, conduct medication review for orthostatic contributors, and recommend home safety modifications
options.C: Increase calcium and vitamin D, and refer to orthopedics for prophylactic hip pinning
options.D: Restrict ambulation; limit patient to one floor of his home to prevent fall events

questions.rationale: Multi-component fall prevention combining exercise (progressive balance and strength training), medication deprescribing of fall-risk medications, and home safety modification is the only intervention with consistent evidence for reducing falls and fall injuries in community-dwelling older adults (CDC STEADI, Cochrane reviews). This patient has multiple modifiable factors: TUG abnormal, sedentary, furosemide may contribute to orthostasis, and specific home hazards identified. Option A is incorrect: hip protectors reduce hip fracture severity after falls but do not prevent falls; calcium and vitamin D are important for bone health but do not reduce fall incidence. Option C is incorrect: prophylactic hip pinning is not a clinical intervention — there is no such procedure for fall prevention; calcium and vitamin D alone do not prevent falls. Option D is incorrect: restricting ambulation accelerates deconditioning, sarcopenia, and functional decline — inactivity is a direct risk factor for future falls and is the opposite of evidence-based care.

questions.stem: An 83-year-old woman with moderate dementia has been prescribed haloperidol 1mg daily by a previous provider for 'agitation at sundowning.' Her family asks whether this medication is appropriate. You review her chart and note she has had two pneumonia hospitalizations in the past 18 months. Which action is most appropriate?

options.A: Continue haloperidol; antipsychotics are the standard of care for dementia-related agitation
options.B: Switch to quetiapine 12.5mg; it has a safer profile than haloperidol in dementia
options.C: Discuss the FDA black box warning with the family; implement non-pharmacological approaches and plan a slow taper of haloperidol
options.D: Increase haloperidol to 2mg if current dose does not fully control agitation

questions.rationale: Antipsychotics in elderly patients with dementia carry an FDA black box warning: increased mortality risk from cardiovascular events (including sudden death) and pneumonia (aspiration). This patient has already had two pneumonia hospitalizations — a red flag for antipsychotic continuation. Non-pharmacological interventions (consistent routines, reduced evening stimulation, caregiver de-escalation techniques) are mandatory first steps. Slow tapering of haloperidol with close monitoring is appropriate. Option A is incorrect: antipsychotics are not 'standard of care' for dementia agitation — they carry a mortality-associated black box warning and should be minimized or avoided whenever possible. Option B is incorrect: switching to quetiapine — also an antipsychotic — does not remove the class-wide black box warning; all atypical and typical antipsychotics carry this risk in dementia patients. Option D is incorrect: increasing the dose in a patient with documented adverse outcomes (two pneumonias) is directly contrary to the FDA black box warning and patient safety principles.

questions.stem: A 79-year-old woman with GERD has been on omeprazole 40mg daily for 6 years. She initially started it after a Helicobacter pylori-associated ulcer, which was eradicated 4 years ago. She has had no GI symptoms for 3 years. Which deprescribing action is most appropriate?

options.A: Continue omeprazole indefinitely; stopping PPIs risks ulcer recurrence in all elderly patients
options.B: Reduce to omeprazole 20mg daily and reassess in 4 weeks
options.C: Attempt PPI tapering or switching to on-demand use; counsel on rebound acid hypersecretion for 2-4 weeks
options.D: Stop omeprazole immediately; transition to calcium carbonate antacids as needed

questions.rationale: This patient no longer has an active indication for high-dose chronic PPI — the H. pylori was eradicated and she has been symptom-free for 3 years. Long-term PPI use has documented harms: C. difficile risk, hypomagnesemia, reduced calcium and B12 absorption (especially relevant in osteoporosis-prone elderly), and increased pneumonia risk. PPI deprescribing is appropriate with patient counseling on expected rebound acid hypersecretion for 2-4 weeks (a common reason patients restart). Tapering or switching to on-demand use is the preferred approach. Option A is incorrect: chronic PPI is not indicated for all elderly patients without active indication; risk-benefit reassessment is mandatory. Option B is incorrect: reducing the dose without a plan to discontinue or use on-demand misses the deprescribing goal; dose reduction alone is an incomplete intervention. Option D is incorrect: abrupt cessation of long-term PPI causes significant rebound acid hypersecretion; the patient is likely to experience severe heartburn and restart the medication — graduated tapering or on-demand transition improves success rates.

questions.stem: An 88-year-old woman with mild dementia and COPD has been taking atorvastatin 40mg for primary cardiovascular prevention for 12 years. She recently transitioned to hospice-level palliative care for advanced COPD. Her daughter asks whether all her medications should continue. In deciding about statin continuation, which consideration is most clinically appropriate?

options.A: Continue atorvastatin; discontinuing statins increases MI risk in elderly patients
options.B: Continue atorvastatin only if LDL is above 100 mg/dL on repeat testing
options.C: Discontinue atorvastatin; in the context of limited life expectancy and palliative goals, the primary prevention benefit timeline will not be realized
options.D: Reduce to atorvastatin 10mg daily to preserve some cardiovascular protection while minimizing cost

questions.rationale: Statins for primary prevention require 3-5 years of continuous use before cardiovascular benefit is realized (NNT). A patient with advanced COPD enrolled in palliative care with limited life expectancy will not live long enough to benefit from primary prevention — the harm (polypharmacy, drug interactions, pill burden, side effects) outweighs the non-existent benefit. Statin discontinuation in this context is supported by STOPP criteria, palliative care guidelines, and goals-of-care principles. This is a shared decision-making conversation with the family. Option A is incorrect: the argument that 'discontinuing increases MI risk' applies to statins for secondary prevention in patients with established ASCVD and adequate life expectancy — not primary prevention in a palliative patient. Option B is incorrect: LDL monitoring for primary prevention statin therapy has no clinical basis here — the issue is goals of care, not lipid levels. Option D is incorrect: reducing the dose neither addresses the goals-of-care question nor removes the polypharmacy burden — a full deprescribing discussion is warranted.

questions.stem: A 76-year-old man with anxiety and insomnia has been taking lorazepam 1mg TID for 8 years. He was started on it after a death in the family and never tapered. He now comes to you as a new patient. He denies any prior taper attempts and says he 'needs' the medication. Physical exam and neurological exam are normal. What is the most important initial step in deprescribing lorazepam in this patient?

options.A: Abruptly discontinue lorazepam; he has been on it for years and should be physiologically dependent
options.B: Switch immediately to diazepam at equivalent dose; it is safer in elderly
options.C: Initiate a very gradual taper (10% dose reduction every 2-4 weeks) while concurrently addressing anxiety with an SSRI and behavioral therapy
options.D: Continue lorazepam; the risk of withdrawal seizures in an 8-year user is too high to attempt tapering

questions.rationale: Long-term benzodiazepine users require slow, gradual tapering — abrupt discontinuation after 8 years risks withdrawal seizures, which can be life-threatening. A 10% dose reduction every 2-4 weeks is a conservative, safe approach. Concurrent treatment of the underlying anxiety (SSRI + behavioral therapy) addresses the indication and reduces relapse risk. Option A is incorrect: abrupt discontinuation of long-term benzodiazepines is dangerous — withdrawal from benzodiazepines resembles alcohol withdrawal (seizures, delirium, autonomic instability) and can be fatal; never abruptly stop after years of use. Option B is incorrect: switching to diazepam in an 80-year-old is problematic — diazepam is a long-acting benzodiazepine with active metabolites that accumulate in elderly patients with reduced hepatic clearance; it is Beers Criteria in older adults. Option D is incorrect: tapering is feasible and recommended even in long-term users — the risk of seizures exists but is managed through slow, monitored tapering, not indefinite continuation of a harmful drug class.

questions.stem: An 82-year-old woman with overactive bladder has been taking oxybutynin 5mg BID for 3 years. She reports adequate bladder control but her daughter has noticed progressive short-term memory decline over the past 18 months. MMSE declined from 28 to 23 over this period. Which medication substitution most appropriately addresses the cognitive concern?

options.A: Switch to tolterodine extended-release; it has lower anticholinergic CNS penetration than oxybutynin
options.B: Switch to mirabegron (a beta-3 adrenergic agonist); it has no anticholinergic activity
options.C: Add rivastigmine (acetylcholinesterase inhibitor) to counteract the anticholinergic effect of oxybutynin
options.D: Continue oxybutynin; memory decline in an 82-year-old is expected age-related change

questions.rationale: Oxybutynin has high CNS anticholinergic penetration and is a known contributor to cognitive decline in elderly patients. Switching to mirabegron — a beta-3 adrenergic agonist — provides equivalent OAB efficacy through a completely different mechanism with no anticholinergic activity, removing the cognitive burden. This is a classic Beers Criteria–driven medication switch. Option A is incorrect: tolterodine ER has lower CNS penetration than oxybutynin but is still a muscarinic antagonist with anticholinergic burden — it reduces but does not eliminate the anticholinergic contribution to cognitive decline. Option C is incorrect: adding an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor to counteract an anticholinergic is irrational polypharmacy — it creates competing pharmacological effects and increases overall drug burden; the correct action is to remove the anticholinergic, not layer another drug on top. Option D is incorrect: the cognitive decline tracked with the medication start and has progressed with continued use; drug-induced cognitive impairment must be investigated before attributing decline to 'normal aging.'

questions.stem: An 80-year-old man with hypertension, CKD stage 3a, and osteoarthritis has been taking naproxen 500mg BID for 6 months prescribed by orthopedics for knee pain. He also takes lisinopril 10mg and chlorthalidone 12.5mg. He presents for follow-up and his creatinine has risen from 1.2 to 1.7 mg/dL over 3 months. Which cascade is most likely responsible and what is the most appropriate action?

options.A: CKD is progressing independently; continue naproxen and increase lisinopril for renoprotection
options.B: Chlorthalidone is causing volume depletion and AKI; reduce the diuretic dose
options.C: NSAID-ACEi-diuretic triple whammy is causing AKI; discontinue naproxen and recheck creatinine
options.D: Add omeprazole and continue naproxen; GI protection will prevent progression

questions.rationale: The 'triple whammy' nephrotoxic combination — NSAID (inhibits prostaglandin-mediated renal afferent arteriolar dilation) + ACEi/ARB (inhibits angiotensin II-mediated efferent constriction) + diuretic (reduces circulating volume) — is the most common cause of reversible AKI in older adults on multiple medications. Naproxen is the identifiable intervention — it was added 6 months ago and the creatinine rise parallels its use. The NSAID must be discontinued. Creatinine will often return to baseline within weeks. Option A is incorrect: continuing naproxen with increased ACEi further compounds the nephrotoxic combination and will worsen AKI. Option B is incorrect: the diuretic is a contributing factor but naproxen is the key precipitant; reducing the diuretic alone without removing the NSAID does not resolve the triple whammy. Option D is incorrect: omeprazole protects the GI tract from NSAID ulceration — it has zero effect on NSAID-induced nephrotoxicity; creatinine rises from renal hemodynamic effects, not GI effects.

questions.stem: A 43-year-old man with moderate psoriasis presents reporting that his ring finger and index finger on both hands have become swollen like 'sausages' and his lower back aches in the morning. His skin disease is currently well-controlled on topical therapy. PASI score is 3. Which action is most appropriate?

options.A: Continue topical therapy; his skin disease is well-controlled and joint symptoms are likely tendinopathy
options.B: Increase topical corticosteroid potency to better control underlying inflammation causing joint symptoms
options.C: Refer to rheumatology regardless of PASI score; dactylitis and axial symptoms suggest psoriatic arthritis
options.D: Start oral methotrexate based on the PASI score alone; it will address both skin and joints

questions.rationale: Dactylitis (diffuse swelling of entire digits — 'sausage fingers') combined with inflammatory back pain (morning stiffness, worse at rest) in a patient with psoriasis is psoriatic arthritis until proven otherwise. PsA affects 30% of psoriasis patients and can develop independently of skin severity — PASI 3 does not rule out PsA. Rheumatology referral is indicated for any suspected psoriatic arthritis regardless of skin PASI. Option A is incorrect: dismissing joint symptoms as tendinopathy misses PsA — tendinopathy does not cause dactylitis (sausage digits are a highly specific PsA sign). Option B is incorrect: increasing topical therapy addresses only the skin; it has no effect on joint inflammation in PsA, which requires systemic treatment. Option D is incorrect: starting methotrexate without rheumatology evaluation is appropriate in some cases but premature as first action — confirmation of PsA diagnosis, exclusion of other arthropathies, and baseline labs should be established with specialist input.

questions.stem: A 7-year-old boy with moderate atopic dermatitis affecting his antecubital fossae and neck presents with his mother. Despite twice-daily hydrocortisone 1% for 8 weeks, the AD has not improved significantly. She asks about a 'stronger cream.' Which management approach is most appropriate?

options.A: Prescribe triamcinolone 0.1% to all affected areas including the neck
options.B: Prescribe tacrolimus 0.03% ointment for face and sensitive areas; upgrade to mid-potency topical CS for trunk and extremity lesions with a consistent emollient regimen
options.C: Refer directly for dupilumab initiation; hydrocortisone failure at 8 weeks indicates moderate-severe disease requiring biologics
options.D: Switch to oral prednisone 1mg/kg for 2 weeks to rapidly clear the flare

questions.rationale: This child's AD is undertreated with hydrocortisone 1% (low potency, appropriate for face only). Step-up therapy for moderate AD in a child requires: tacrolimus 0.03% ointment (pediatric dose — not 0.1%) for sensitive areas (neck, eyelids, face) where steroids risk skin atrophy; and mid-potency topical corticosteroids (triamcinolone 0.05% or desonide) for trunk and extremity lesions. Consistent emollient use is the foundation. Option A is incorrect: triamcinolone 0.1% (mid-high potency) should not be applied to the neck in children — this area has thin skin with high absorption; risk of atrophy and hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal suppression is significant. Option C is incorrect: dupilumab is indicated for inadequate response to APPROPRIATE topical therapy — hydrocortisone 1% is not adequate, not 'failed appropriate topical treatment'; step up first, then consider biologic. Option D is incorrect: oral corticosteroids cause AD rebound after discontinuation, promote skin barrier impairment, and HPA suppression in children — short courses do not achieve remission and are not recommended.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old woman with plaque psoriasis has failed adequate trials of topical corticosteroids and calcipotriol. Her PASI score is 13 and DLQI is 14. She asks about biologic therapy. Which response most accurately reflects the biologic referral criteria?

options.A: She does not qualify; biologic therapy requires failure of phototherapy before systemic or biologic agents
options.B: She qualifies; PASI >10 and DLQI >10 with failure of topical therapy meets standard biologic referral criteria
options.C: She qualifies only if she has psoriatic arthritis; skin-only disease cannot receive biologics
options.D: She qualifies; however, biologics cannot be started until methotrexate has been tried for at least 6 months

questions.rationale: PASI (Psoriasis Area and Severity Index) >10 and DLQI (Dermatology Life Quality Index) >10 together, with failure of adequate topical therapy, meet standard criteria for biologic referral. This patient meets both thresholds. PASI alone >10 or DLQI alone >10 with topical failure is sufficient in many guidelines. Option A is incorrect: while phototherapy is an option in the stepwise ladder, it is not mandatory before biologic referral in all guidelines; patients with PASI/DLQI scores meeting thresholds can be referred for biologic consideration after topical failure. Option C is incorrect: biologics (IL-17, IL-23, TNF-alpha inhibitors) are FDA-approved for moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis regardless of joint involvement. Option D is incorrect: methotrexate is an option in the stepwise ladder but is not a mandatory prerequisite for biologic referral in all cases — some guidelines allow biologic first after topical failure in patients who cannot tolerate or have contraindications to conventional systemics.

questions.stem: A 14-year-old girl with severe atopic dermatitis presents to urgent care with a rapidly spreading rash. Over 24 hours, punched-out, monomorphic crusted erosions have spread from her usual AD distribution to her face, trunk, and arms. She has a fever of 38.6°C and appears ill. Her mother applied extra topical steroid cream last night. What is the most appropriate management?

options.A: Prescribe a medium-potency topical corticosteroid and oral antihistamine for acute AD flare
options.B: Diagnose eczema herpeticum; start systemic acyclovir and discontinue topical steroid
options.C: Start oral prednisone for severe atopic dermatitis flare with secondary infection
options.D: Prescribe oral antibiotic (cephalexin) for presumed staphylococcal superinfection of eczema

questions.rationale: Eczema herpeticum is HSV superinfection of atopic dermatitis — a dermatological emergency. The hallmark features are: punched-out, monomorphic (all the same size and stage) crusted vesicular erosions, rapid spread beyond typical AD distribution, fever, and systemic illness. Topical corticosteroids applied to HSV eczema herpeticum suppress local immunity and can cause catastrophic HSV dissemination. Acyclovir 400mg 5x/day (or valacyclovir 1g TID) must be started immediately, and topical steroids must be discontinued. Hospitalizing for IV acyclovir should be considered for widespread disease. Option A is incorrect: topical corticosteroids will worsen HSV dissemination — this is dangerous and contraindicated in eczema herpeticum. Option C is incorrect: oral prednisone is immunosuppressive and would compound the iatrogenic immunosuppression already caused by the topical steroid application; it is absolutely contraindicated in active viral infection. Option D is incorrect: while staphylococcal superinfection (impetiginization) is common in AD, it presents with honey-crusted lesions, not monomorphic punched-out erosions — the clinical pattern here is diagnostic for HSV.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with moderate-to-severe psoriasis is started on methotrexate 15mg weekly by her dermatologist. Which medication should be prescribed concurrently to reduce the most common serious side effects of methotrexate?

options.A: Leucovorin 5mg weekly 24 hours after each methotrexate dose
options.B: Folic acid 1mg daily on non-methotrexate days
options.C: Calcium 1000mg daily to prevent osteoporosis
options.D: Pyridoxine 50mg daily to reduce peripheral neuropathy risk

questions.rationale: Folic acid 1mg daily (typically on days not taking methotrexate, though some protocols allow all days) is standard practice with methotrexate to reduce the most common side effects: mucositis, hepatotoxicity, and cytopenias. Folate supplementation reduces these adverse effects without significantly compromising methotrexate's therapeutic efficacy. Option A is incorrect: leucovorin (folinic acid) is used for methotrexate rescue in oncology overdose situations — it actively rescues cells from methotrexate inhibition and would reduce psoriasis efficacy if given weekly alongside therapeutic doses. Option C is incorrect: calcium supplementation does not address methotrexate toxicity — it is relevant for corticosteroid-induced osteoporosis but not methotrexate management. Option D is incorrect: pyridoxine is used for isoniazid-induced peripheral neuropathy and chemotherapy regimens — it is not standard practice with low-dose weekly methotrexate for psoriasis.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents for a well visit and smokes 1 pack per day. She states she is 'not ready to quit right now.' She is not interested in pharmacotherapy. Which response best represents evidence-based counseling for a precontemplation patient?

options.A: Document her refusal and plan to address it again in 1 year at her next annual visit
options.B: Use the 5R framework: explore personal Relevance, Risks, Rewards, Roadblocks, and repeat the conversation at every visit
options.C: Refer to a smoking cessation specialist; readiness counseling is outside the NP scope
options.D: Prescribe a nicotine patch without her agreement to avoid withdrawal-related harms

questions.rationale: For patients not ready to quit, the 5R framework is the evidence-based counseling approach: Relevance (personal health and family context), Risks (health consequences specific to her), Rewards (benefits of quitting), Roadblocks (barriers to quitting), and Repetition (returning to the topic at every visit). Brief motivational counseling at every visit significantly increases quit rates over time. Option A is incorrect: addressing tobacco use only at annual visits misses the opportunity to plant a seed at every encounter; brief counseling at every visit is proven more effective than annual discussion. Option C is incorrect: brief motivational counseling for smoking cessation is within every primary care NP's scope — this is core preventive medicine. Option D is incorrect: prescribing any medication without the patient's agreement and informed consent is unethical and a boundary violation; furthermore, an unrestricted NRT patch in a full-time smoker without a quit plan would be misused.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with a 30 pack-year history wants to quit smoking. He has a history of major depressive disorder, currently stable on sertraline 100mg. He asks whether he can use varenicline. How should you counsel him regarding its safety in patients with psychiatric history?

options.A: Varenicline is contraindicated in any patient with a history of depression or psychiatric illness
options.B: Varenicline can be used with caution; the EAGLES trial showed no significant increase in neuropsychiatric adverse events compared to placebo in patients with stable psychiatric conditions
options.C: Switch his sertraline to bupropion, which doubles as an antidepressant and smoking cessation aid
options.D: Recommend NRT only; all non-NRT cessation aids are unsafe in patients on SSRIs

questions.rationale: The EAGLES trial (2016) definitively evaluated neuropsychiatric safety of varenicline and bupropion versus NRT and placebo in patients with and without stable psychiatric illness. The trial found no significant increase in serious neuropsychiatric adverse events in patients with stable psychiatric conditions. Varenicline remains the most effective cessation agent and can be used in patients with controlled psychiatric disorders, including stable depression, with appropriate monitoring. Option A is incorrect: this was the former FDA concern prior to the EAGLES trial — the black box warning for neuropsychiatric adverse events was subsequently removed after EAGLES demonstrated safety. Option C is incorrect: switching sertraline to bupropion for the dual purpose is complex and risks destabilizing well-controlled depression; combining bupropion (NDRI) with sertraline (SSRI) also increases serotonin activity; this is not the recommended approach. Option D is incorrect: NRT is safe in combination with SSRIs, but restricting cessation pharmacotherapy to NRT only for all psychiatric patients is overly restrictive and not evidence-based post-EAGLES.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman with CKD stage 4 (CrCl 22 mL/min calculated by Cockcroft-Gault) and 35 pack-years of smoking wants to quit. She asks about varenicline. What is the most appropriate prescribing decision?

options.A: Prescribe standard varenicline 0.5mg daily → 1mg BID; renal function does not affect varenicline dosing
options.B: Prescribe varenicline with renal dose reduction: start 0.5mg daily, maximum 0.5mg BID for CrCl <30
options.C: Avoid varenicline entirely in CKD; use bupropion SR as a safer alternative
options.D: Avoid all pharmacotherapy; recommend cessation counseling only for patients with CrCl <30

questions.rationale: Varenicline is renally cleared and requires dose adjustment when CrCl <30 mL/min: start at 0.5mg once daily, with a maximum dose of 0.5mg BID (never titrate to the standard 1mg BID in this population due to drug accumulation risk). This patient's CrCl by Cockcroft-Gault is 22 mL/min — below the 30 threshold. She can and should use varenicline for the highest-efficacy cessation strategy, with the renal-adjusted dose. Option A is incorrect: renal function significantly affects varenicline — the drug is approximately 90% renally cleared; standard dosing would lead to accumulation and increased adverse effect risk (nausea, neuropsychiatric). Option C is incorrect: bupropion is also renally cleared (to some extent) and carries its own risks (seizure threshold lowering); avoiding varenicline when the correct renal dose adjustment exists is unnecessarily restrictive. Option D is incorrect: patients with CKD benefit from smoking cessation at least as much as the general population (smoking accelerates CKD progression); withholding pharmacotherapy from a highly motivated patient with CKD is inappropriate.

questions.stem: A 44-year-old man with a history of alcohol use disorder (currently sober 3 years) and epilepsy managed with levetiracetam wants to quit smoking. He asks about bupropion SR. Which response is most clinically appropriate?

options.A: Bupropion SR is safe in patients with epilepsy on stable anticonvulsants; proceed with standard dosing
options.B: Bupropion SR is contraindicated in patients with a seizure disorder; use varenicline instead
options.C: Bupropion SR is contraindicated due to alcohol history; NRT is the only safe option
options.D: Bupropion SR is safe in patients sober for 3 years; dose-dependent seizure risk does not apply to controlled epilepsy

questions.rationale: Bupropion SR has a dose-dependent seizure risk and is specifically contraindicated in patients with a seizure disorder (epilepsy), regardless of current anticonvulsant control. Even on levetiracetam, the additional seizure threshold lowering from bupropion represents an unacceptable risk. Varenicline is the appropriate alternative — it does not affect seizure threshold and is the most effective cessation agent. Option A is incorrect: epilepsy is an absolute contraindication to bupropion SR regardless of anticonvulsant therapy — adding bupropion creates pharmacodynamic risk regardless of concurrent AED use. Option C is incorrect: the relevant contraindication here is epilepsy, not alcohol use history; past alcohol use disorder (sober 3 years) is not a contraindication to bupropion — the seizure risk from abrupt alcohol withdrawal (acute intoxication context) is the relevant alcohol concern, which does not apply to a patient 3 years sober. Option D is incorrect: dose-dependent seizure risk from bupropion applies to patients with epilepsy regardless of controlled status — 'controlled epilepsy' on AEDs does not remove the contraindication.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman at 16 weeks gestation smokes 10 cigarettes daily and is highly motivated to quit. She is concerned about using any medications during pregnancy. What is the most accurate counseling and recommended approach?

options.A: Advise her to continue smoking at the same rate; stopping abruptly in mid-pregnancy may harm the fetus
options.B: Recommend behavioral counseling alone; all pharmacotherapy is absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy
options.C: Offer NRT (patch plus short-acting form) as the preferred pharmacotherapy; continued smoking causes significantly greater fetal harm than NRT nicotine exposure
options.D: Prescribe varenicline; it is the safest and most effective option for highly motivated pregnant patients

questions.rationale: Nicotine replacement therapy (NRT) is the preferred smoking cessation pharmacotherapy in pregnancy. The key counseling point: the toxins from cigarette combustion (carbon monoxide, 4,000+ chemicals) cause significantly more fetal harm — including IUGR, placental abruption, stillbirth, and SIDS — than NRT's nicotine alone. Combination NRT (patch for baseline control + gum or lozenge for cravings) is safe and effective. Avoid overnight extended-release patches in the 3rd trimester. Option A is incorrect: advising a motivated patient to continue smoking in pregnancy is harmful — the goal is complete cessation. Option B is incorrect: behavioral counseling alone is less effective than combined pharmacotherapy + counseling in pregnancy; NRT is recommended when counseling alone is insufficient. Option D is incorrect: varenicline has insufficient safety data in pregnancy — animal studies show adverse effects and there is no adequate human RCT data; it is not recommended for use in pregnancy; NRT has the most established safety profile.
